Which finding requires immediate correction?
choices: All three: the unsupported span, the tight bend (exceeds minimum bend radius), and the proximity to the heater duct (heat source violation)
Only the 18-inch unsupported span — this exceeds the typical 12-inch maximum
Only the proximity to the heat duct — the other findings are within normal tolerances
Only the tight bend — unsupported spans and heat proximity are acceptable in GA aircraft
practice.explanation: All three are installation defects: unsupported spans can sag and chafe, tight bends damage conductors, and proximity to heat sources degrades insulation. All must be corrected.

preassessment.question: What must be maintained when routing a wire harness in an aircraft?
choices: Only regular clamp spacing
Regular support, clearance from hazards, and no sharp bends
Only clearance from moving parts
Only minimum bend radius

questions.learningObjective: Harness securing methods: lacing cord, nylon tie wraps, cushioned clamps
questions.fullObjective: State that acceptable methods for securing wire harnesses in aircraft include approved MS or AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protectors, or cushioned clamps at intervals specified in the maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: Acceptable aircraft harness securing methods: (1) approved MS/AN lacing cord (traditional military and civilian method), (2) nylon tie wraps with edge protectors where needed, (3) cushioned clamps (AdEL or equivalent) at intervals per the maintenance manual.
flashcard.analogy: Three approved ways to bundle wires — lacing cord is like tying shoelaces, tie wraps are like twist ties, and cushioned clamps are like padded brackets. All must be approved types at correct spacing.

jeopardy.question: Three acceptable methods for securing aircraft wire harnesses are: approved lacing cord, cushioned clamps at specified intervals, and this method with edge protectors where needed.
choices: Automotive spiral wrap
Metal cable trays
Electrical tape wrapping
Nylon tie wraps
jeopardy.explanation: Nylon tie wraps (with edge protection where the harness contacts structure) are an approved harness securing method along with lacing cord and cushioned AdEL-type clamps.

multipleChoice.question: Which methods are acceptable for securing wire harnesses in an aircraft?
choices: Approved MS/AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protection, or cushioned clamps at intervals per the maintenance manual
Rubber bands and cable loops — they avoid contact damage to the outer jacket
Plastic clips from automotive harness kits — these provide adequate support
Electrical tape wrapping at 12-inch intervals — provides both securing and insulation
multipleChoice.explanation: Three approved methods: lacing cord (MS/AN specs), nylon tie wraps (with edge protection), and cushioned clamps per the manual. Automotive hardware, rubber bands, and electrical tape are not approved.

practice.question: A technician is securing a newly fabricated wire harness in a Cessna. The maintenance manual does not specify a specific securing interval in this zone. What securing methods are acceptable?
choices: Electrical tape at 6-inch intervals to keep the harness compact
Approved MS/AN lacing cord, or nylon tie wraps with edge protection where the bundle contacts sharp structure, or cushioned clamps — all at approximately the standard interval
Spiral wrap conduit from the automotive supply store — equivalent protection to aviation clamps
No securing needed if the harness is tensioned by connector placement
practice.explanation: When the manual doesn't specify interval, use standard practice (~12 inches) with approved methods: MS/AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protection, or cushioned clamps.

preassessment.question: Which is an acceptable method for securing an aircraft wire harness?
choices: Electrical tape
Rubber bands
Approved nylon tie wraps with edge protection
Automotive spiral wrap

questions.learningObjective: Service loop provides extra wire for future re-termination or repair
questions.fullObjective: Explain the purpose of a service loop in an aircraft wire harness: to provide extra wire length at connectors and terminal points to allow for future re-termination, adjustment, or repair without splicing.

flashcard.definition: A service loop is extra wire length coiled or folded near a connector or terminal, providing material for future re-terminations (each strip-and-crimp operation shortens the wire slightly) — preventing the need for splices when a connector is rebuilt over the aircraft's service life.
flashcard.analogy: A service loop is like leaving slack in a guitar string before tuning — you need extra material to wrap the tuning peg. Without it, you can't tune without splicing more string.

jeopardy.question: The extra wire coiled near a connector in a harness installation is called a service loop; its purpose is to allow re-termination or repair in the future without this costly fix.
choices: Splicing in additional wire
Running an entirely new wire from the equipment
Shortening the connector contact positions
Replacing the entire harness
jeopardy.explanation: Service loops provide wire for future re-terminations. Without a service loop, stripping and re-terminating a wire eventually uses up all the length, requiring a splice to add more wire.

multipleChoice.question: What is the purpose of a service loop in an aircraft wire harness?
choices: To allow the harness to flex during aircraft structural flexing without breaking
To provide extra wire length near connectors for future re-termination or repair without the need for splicing
To distribute the harness weight evenly by creating balanced coils in the bundle
To increase the harness resistance so that each circuit is individually tuned
multipleChoice.explanation: Service loops store extra wire near connectors. Each time a connector is re-terminated (pins replaced, damaged section cut back), the service loop provides material. Without it, the wire becomes too short and a splice must be added.

practice.question: During harness fabrication, a technician skips the service loops at all connector ends to save installation time. What problem will this cause during future maintenance?
choices: The harness will be heavier due to the extra wire coils
Service loops are optional and their omission has no maintenance impact

When connectors need re-termination in the future, there will be no spare wire — each re-crimp shortens the wire slightly, and without service loops, the wire will eventually become too short, requiring a splice
Without service loops, the harness cannot be secured at correct intervals
practice.explanation: Every re-crimp shortens the wire by stripping and trimming back. Without service loops, the wire eventually reaches the routing hardware and can no longer be re-terminated without adding a splice — service loops are a long-term maintainability investment.

preassessment.question: Why are service loops included in aircraft wire harnesses?
choices: To reduce harness weight
To provide electrical filtering
To distribute harness rigidity
To allow future re-termination without splicing

questions.learningObjective: Wire bundles near flight controls: conduit with slack, prevent contact
questions.fullObjective: Apply the best practice for wire bundles routed near moving flight controls: use approved conduit or spiral wrap with sufficient slack, and secure the bundle so it cannot contact or restrict control movement.

flashcard.definition: Wire bundles routed near flight control cables or linkages must be protected with approved conduit or spiral wrap AND have sufficient slack to prevent the bundle from being pulled taut or restricting control movement at any control surface position.
flashcard.analogy: Like giving a leash enough length so it doesn't choke the dog when it turns — the bundle needs enough slack so that full control deflection doesn't stretch, pinch, or break the wires.

jeopardy.question: Wire bundles routed near moving flight controls must be installed with approved conduit or spiral wrap AND this must be provided to prevent restriction of control movement.
choices: Anti-chafe tape
Sufficient slack
Additional ground bonding
Heat shrink tubing
jeopardy.explanation: Slack is essential — a tight bundle near flight controls can be pinched or stretched when the control moves through its full range, risking wire damage or worse, restricting control movement.

multipleChoice.question: What is required when routing a wire bundle near moving flight control linkages?
choices: Route the bundle as close as possible to the controls to minimize length
Attach the bundle directly to the flight control cable to move with it
Use approved conduit or spiral wrap and provide sufficient slack so the bundle cannot contact or restrict control movement at any position
Route the bundle on the opposite side of the fuselage from the flight controls
multipleChoice.explanation: Bundles near flight controls must be protected (conduit/spiral wrap) and have sufficient slack so they cannot bind, pinch, or restrict the controls through their full deflection range — a flight safety requirement.

practice.question: An inspector finds a wire bundle routed parallel to the aileron bellcrank with only 1/8-inch clearance and no conduit protection. At full deflection, the bellcrank touches the bundle. What must be corrected?
choices: Apply heat-shrink tubing over the affected bundle section and add a note to the logbook
The 1/8-inch clearance is acceptable — only direct contact at neutral position is prohibited
Secure the bundle tighter to the structure so it cannot contact the bellcrank

Re-route the bundle with adequate clearance at all control positions, add approved conduit or spiral wrap for protection, and ensure sufficient slack so the bundle cannot contact the bellcrank at any position
practice.explanation: Any wire contact with flight control hardware is a flight safety hazard. Re-routing with adequate clearance, conduit protection, and sufficient slack at all control positions is required.

preassessment.question: What must be provided when routing wire bundles near flight controls?
choices: Conduit or spiral wrap with sufficient slack
Direct attachment to the control cable
Anti-chafe tape only
Maximum tension with zero slack

questions.learningObjective: Chafed wire bundle: remove power, assess damage, follow approved repair procedure
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire bundle damaged by chafing against a metal edge, determine the correct first step: remove power, assess the full extent of damage, and follow approved repair procedures from the maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: When a wire bundle is found chafed by a metal edge: (1) remove all power from affected circuits, (2) assess the full extent of damage (examine the entire contact area, not just the visible portion), (3) follow only approved repair procedures from the applicable maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13.
flashcard.analogy: Discovering a chafed wire is like finding a gas leak — first shut off the valve (remove power), then assess how much pipe is damaged (extent of damage), then use the approved repair method (not duct tape).

jeopardy.question: When a wire bundle is found chafed against a metal edge, the first step is to remove power, then assess the full extent of damage, and then follow only approved procedures from this document.
choices: The aircraft weight and balance report
The aircraft's airworthiness certificate
The applicable maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13
The avionics shop's standard operating procedure
jeopardy.explanation: Approved repair procedures from the maintenance manual or AC 43.13 are the only acceptable methods for repairing chafed wiring. Unapproved repairs render the aircraft unairworthy.

multipleChoice.question: A wire bundle is found chafed against a structural member during an inspection. What is the correct procedure?
choices: Wrap the chafed area with electrical tape and add a note to the next inspection
Apply heat shrink tubing over the chafed section and mark the installation as repaired
Reroute the bundle immediately without removing power to minimize aircraft downtime
Remove power from all affected circuits, assess the full extent of damage, and repair using approved procedures from the maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13
multipleChoice.explanation: Power removal prevents arcing during assessment. Full damage assessment reveals the true extent. Only approved repair procedures (maintenance manual, AC 43.13) ensure the repair meets airworthiness standards.

practice.question: During a pre-flight inspection, a pilot reports a burnt smell near the instrument panel. Investigation reveals a wire bundle has chafed through to bare conductor against a sharp bracket. What are the correct steps in order?
choices: Remove electrical power from all potentially affected circuits, assess the full extent of chafe damage along the entire contact area, and repair per the maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13 — do not return to service until repair is complete and inspected
Apply vinyl tape immediately to prevent further arcing, then assess the full damage
Reroute the bundle past the bracket and restore power to check if the smell is gone
Replace only the visibly damaged wire and restore power for a ground test before flight
practice.explanation: Power removal first (safety), then full damage assessment (not just visible portion), then approved repair only. Returning to service before completing an approved repair is not airworthy.

preassessment.question: What is the first action when discovering a chafed wire bundle?
choices: Apply tape to the damaged area
Remove power from affected circuits
Reroute the bundle immediately
Call the aircraft owner for instructions

questions.learningObjective: Nicked coaxial center conductor: replace the entire cable run
questions.fullObjective: Given a coaxial cable center conductor found nicked, determine the correct action: replace the entire cable run because a nicked center conductor compromises the cable's impedance and structural integrity.

flashcard.definition: A nicked coaxial cable center conductor must result in complete cable replacement — not repair. A nick changes the local impedance (causing signal reflection), reduces mechanical strength (causing fatigue failure), and cannot be reliably repaired to original specification.
flashcard.analogy: A nicked coaxial conductor is like a cracked violin string — you don't patch it, you replace it. Any nick changes the vibration characteristics (impedance) and creates a weak point (fatigue).

jeopardy.question: A nicked coaxial cable center conductor requires complete cable replacement because the nick creates a local impedance change and compromises this property of the conductor.
choices: Electrical resistance rating
Shield coverage percentage
Temperature rating of the insulation
Structural integrity and mechanical strength
jeopardy.explanation: A nick reduces the center conductor cross-section, compromising mechanical strength (vibration fatigue) and changing local impedance (causing signal reflections). Neither is repairable — the run must be replaced.

multipleChoice.question: An avionics technician finds a nick in the center conductor of an NAV/COM antenna coaxial cable during inspection. What is the correct action?
choices: Replace the entire cable run — a nicked center conductor compromises both impedance and mechanical integrity and cannot be reliably repaired
Solder fill the nick to restore conductor cross-section, then continue using the cable
Wrap the nicked area with coaxial repair tape and conduct a VSWR check before approving the cable
Trim the cable back to the nick point and install a coaxial splice connector
multipleChoice.explanation: A nicked coaxial center conductor cannot be reliably repaired. The nick changes local impedance (creating reflections that degrade RF performance) and reduces mechanical strength (promoting fatigue failure). Replace the run.

practice.question: During a pre-installation inspection of a new COM antenna cable, a technician finds a small nick in the center conductor near the mid-span. The cable is otherwise undamaged. Can it be installed?
choices: Yes — the nick is small and the cable will work normally for most frequencies
No — the nicked center conductor compromises impedance and structural integrity; the cable must be replaced with an undamaged one before installation
Yes — apply solder to fill the nick and cover with heat-shrink before installation
Yes — check VSWR after installation; if below 2:1, the cable is acceptable
practice.explanation: A nicked conductor should never be installed. The nick creates an impedance discontinuity that degrades RF performance and is a fatigue failure point. Use an undamaged cable.

preassessment.question: What action is required when a coaxial cable center conductor is found nicked?
choices: Solder fill the nick
Cover with heat shrink and test
Replace the entire cable run
Install a mid-span splice

questions.learningObjective: Damaged harness jacket: assess damage and repair per manual or replace if severe
questions.fullObjective: Given a harness with a damaged outer jacket, determine the correct corrective action: assess the extent of damage and repair using approved heat-shrink or conduit per the maintenance manual, or replace the harness if damage is severe.

flashcard.definition: A damaged harness outer jacket requires a damage assessment: minor/localized damage may be repaired with approved heat-shrink tubing or conduit per the maintenance manual; extensive, widespread, or damage reaching individual wire insulation typically requires harness replacement.
flashcard.analogy: Like assessing a damaged car bumper: a small dent gets repaired; a bumper that has caved into the engine compartment gets replaced. The severity determines the action.

jeopardy.question: When a wire harness outer jacket is damaged, the correct response depends on extent of damage: minor damage allows heat-shrink or conduit repair per the manual, but severe damage requires this.
choices: Harness replacement
Application of vinyl tape over the damaged section
Installation of a metal shield over the damaged area
A one-time logbook notation
jeopardy.explanation: Severe outer jacket damage — especially if individual wire insulation is compromised — cannot be repaired to original airworthiness. The harness must be replaced.

multipleChoice.question: A wire harness outer jacket is found damaged during an inspection. What is the correct approach?
choices: Always replace the entire harness regardless of the extent of damage
Assess the extent of damage: repair with approved heat-shrink or conduit per the maintenance manual for localized damage, or replace the harness if damage is severe or widespread
Apply vinyl tape over any damaged section — this is the standard field repair method
Damage to the outer jacket only is not an airworthiness concern — document and monitor
multipleChoice.explanation: Assessment is the key step. Not all jacket damage requires harness replacement — minor, localized damage may be repaired per the maintenance manual using heat-shrink or conduit. Severe damage requires replacement.

practice.question: Inspection reveals the outer jacket of a wiring harness has been chafed through over a 4-inch section, exposing multiple individual wire insulations. Two wires show visible insulation scoring. What is the correct action?
choices: Repair the outer jacket with heat-shrink tubing and return to service
Wrap the entire section with vinyl electrical tape and note in the logbook

Assess the individual wire insulation damage — with two wires showing scoring, the harness has been compromised beyond acceptable repair limits and replacement is required, not just outer jacket repair
Apply conduit over the section and document as a permanent repair
practice.explanation: When individual wire insulations are damaged (scoring), the harness integrity is compromised beyond what an outer jacket repair addresses. Harness replacement is the correct action when damage reaches individual wires.

preassessment.question: When should a damaged wire harness be replaced rather than repaired?
choices: Never — always repair per the manual
Always replace any harness with outer jacket damage
When outer jacket damage exceeds 1 inch in length
When damage is severe or affects individual wire insulation

questions.learningObjective: Center punch creates guide indentation to prevent drill wandering
questions.fullObjective: Explain the primary purpose of a center punch in an avionics repair shop: creating a precise indentation on metal to guide a drill bit and prevent it from wandering during drilling.

flashcard.definition: A center punch is a hardened steel tool struck with a hammer to create a precise conical indentation in metal; the indentation seats the drill bit tip, preventing it from skating across the work surface and ensuring accurate hole placement.
flashcard.analogy: A center punch is like making a thumbprint in clay before poking a hole — the depression gives the drill bit a starting point so it doesn't slide sideways before cutting.

jeopardy.question: In an avionics repair shop, this hardened tool is struck with a hammer to create a guide indentation that prevents a drill bit from wandering on metal.
choices: Center punch
Scribe
Drift punch
Prick punch
jeopardy.explanation: The center punch creates a conical seat for the drill bit tip, preventing lateral movement (wandering) before the drill cuts through the metal surface.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of a center punch in an avionics repair shop?
choices: To remove stuck fasteners by driving them from the back side
To create a precise indentation that guides the drill bit and prevents wandering during drilling
To mark scribe lines on sheet metal for cutting
To test the hardness of a metal surface before drilling
multipleChoice.explanation: A center punch creates an indentation that seats the drill tip, preventing it from sliding when drilling begins. Without this, the drill wanders and holes are misplaced.

practice.question: A technician needs to drill a hole in an avionics rack panel at a precise location for a new indicator lamp. What is the correct first step before drilling?
choices: Apply masking tape to the panel and drill through the tape for precision
Start the drill at slow speed and walk it to the correct location
Mark the exact location with a scribe, then use a center punch to create a guide indentation before drilling
Use the drill bit to scratch a starting mark before applying full pressure
practice.explanation: Scribe the location for accuracy, then center punch to create the drill guide indentation. This two-step process ensures the drill starts and stays at the correct position.

preassessment.question: Why is a center punch used before drilling metal?
choices: To test metal hardness
To remove stuck fasteners
To mark scribe lines
To prevent drill bit wandering

questions.learningObjective: Oscilloscope visualizes voltage waveforms showing amplitude, frequency, and shape
questions.fullObjective: Explain that an oscilloscope is used to visualize voltage waveforms over time, allowing a technician to observe signal amplitude, frequency, and waveform shape.

flashcard.definition: An oscilloscope displays voltage as a function of time on a calibrated screen, allowing observation of signal amplitude (peak voltage), frequency (cycles per second), waveform shape (sine, square, triangle, digital), and timing relationships between signals.
flashcard.analogy: An oscilloscope is like a video camera for electricity — it shows you exactly what a voltage signal is doing over time, revealing information that a simple voltmeter can only partially capture.

jeopardy.question: This test instrument allows a technician to visualize voltage waveforms over time, observing signal amplitude, frequency, and shape on a calibrated display.
choices: Digital multimeter
Oscilloscope
Bus analyzer
Frequency counter
jeopardy.explanation: An oscilloscope displays voltage vs. time, enabling measurement and visualization of amplitude, frequency, and waveform shape — information that a DMM cannot provide.

multipleChoice.question: What information can an oscilloscope provide that a digital multimeter cannot?
choices: Precise DC voltage measurements to six decimal places
Direct measurement of circuit resistance using a four-wire method
Visual display of signal waveform shape, amplitude over time, and frequency
Current measurement without breaking the circuit
multipleChoice.explanation: An oscilloscope shows voltage vs. time — waveform shape, amplitude changes, frequency, and timing. A DMM gives only a single steady-state value; it cannot show signal dynamics or waveform characteristics.

practice.question: An avionics technician suspects the output of a sensor is clipping (flat-topping) at high values. Which tool should be used to confirm this?
choices: A digital multimeter set to AC volts to measure RMS output
A frequency counter to measure the sensor's output update rate
A bus analyzer to capture the data words produced by the sensor
An oscilloscope to visualize the waveform and observe whether the peaks are clipped flat
practice.explanation: Clipping — where the waveform peaks are truncated — is a waveform shape characteristic visible only on an oscilloscope. A DMM would show an average or RMS value but would not reveal the clipping.

preassessment.question: What does an oscilloscope display?
choices: Voltage waveforms showing amplitude, frequency, and shape
Resistance values over time
Bus data words
Current flow direction

questions.learningObjective: OL on multimeter ohms = open circuit or out of range
questions.fullObjective: State that 'OL' on a multimeter set to ohms means the measured resistance exceeds the meter's range, indicating an open circuit or resistance too high to measure on the selected scale.

flashcard.definition: 'OL' (over limit or overload) on a multimeter set to ohms means the resistance is greater than the meter's maximum measurable range on the selected scale — typically indicating an open circuit (broken wire, open fuse, or disconnected path) or a very high resistance.
flashcard.analogy: OL is like a bathroom scale reading 'ERR' when an elephant stands on it — the scale's range is exceeded. OL means the resistance is beyond the meter's limit, most often because the circuit is open (no path for current).

jeopardy.question: When a multimeter set to ohms displays 'OL', this is the most likely circuit condition being indicated.
choices: A perfectly balanced resistance equal to the meter's full-scale reading
A short circuit reading below the meter's minimum range
An open circuit or resistance exceeding the meter's selected range
A reverse-biased diode in the measurement path
jeopardy.explanation: 'OL' means the resistance exceeds the meter's range — typically an open circuit (infinite resistance) or a legitimate high-resistance reading that exceeds the selected scale.

multipleChoice.question: A multimeter set to the 200-ohm range shows 'OL' when connected across a wire. What does this indicate?
choices: The wire has exactly 200 ohms resistance — the display switches to OL at full scale
The measurement polarity is reversed and the leads must be swapped
The multimeter is damaged and cannot measure the circuit correctly
The resistance is greater than the 200-ohm range, most likely an open circuit (broken wire) or very high resistance
multipleChoice.explanation: 'OL' on the 200-ohm range means resistance exceeds 200 ohms. For a wire, this almost certainly means an open circuit — no conductive path exists between the probe points.

practice.question: A technician checks a suspected broken wire for continuity using a multimeter on the 200-ohm range. The display shows 'OL'. What does this tell the technician?
choices: The wire resistance exceeds the meter's 200-ohm range, strongly indicating an open circuit — a break in the wire or an open connection
The wire has 0 ohms — OL is the code for a good continuity reading
The wire has exactly 200 ohms resistance, confirming it is within normal tolerance
The multimeter leads are shorted — the test is invalid
practice.explanation: OL on the ohms range means no measurable resistance path — the wire has an open circuit. The technician should now locate the break by checking sections of the wire individually.

preassessment.question: What does 'OL' mean on a multimeter set to ohms?
choices: Optimal level
Open circuit or over-range
Output load
Ohm limit reached maximum

questions.learningObjective: Digital databus analyzer captures, displays, and decodes bus traffic to verify and diagnose
questions.fullObjective: Explain the primary purpose of a digital databus analyzer in a GA avionics shop: capturing, displaying, and decoding bus traffic to verify correct data transmission and diagnose communication faults.

flashcard.definition: A digital databus (protocol) analyzer connects non-intrusively to a databus and captures, timestamps, and decodes bus traffic in real time — displaying individual data words with their labels, values, and error conditions, enabling verification of correct operation and diagnosis of communication faults.
flashcard.analogy: A databus analyzer is like a flight data recorder for the ARINC 429 bus — it captures everything that flows by, decodes it into readable form, and helps you understand what happened when things went wrong.

jeopardy.question: This test instrument captures, displays, and decodes ARINC 429 bus traffic in real time, enabling a technician to verify correct data transmission and identify communication faults.
choices: Oscilloscope
Frequency counter
Digital multimeter
Digital databus analyzer
jeopardy.explanation: A databus analyzer decodes the bus protocol layer — showing individual ARINC 429 labels, values, timing, and errors — unlike an oscilloscope which shows only the electrical waveform without decoding.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary function of a digital databus analyzer in avionics maintenance?
choices: To capture, display, and decode bus traffic to verify correct data transmission and diagnose communication faults
To measure the DC power supply voltage to each avionics LRU
To program ARINC 429 LRUs with their initial configuration settings
To measure the resistance of the bus termination resistors at each end
multipleChoice.explanation: A databus analyzer provides non-intrusive, real-time decoding of bus protocol data — showing what labels are being transmitted, their values, timing, and any errors — the essential tool for ARINC 429 and MIL-STD-1553 diagnosis.

practice.question: An autopilot is not responding to altitude data from the air data computer. Wiring continuity is confirmed. What is the next most useful diagnostic step?
choices: Replace the ADC with a known-good unit to verify the fault by substitution
Connect a databus analyzer to the ARINC 429 bus between the ADC and autopilot to verify that the ADC is transmitting the altitude label with correct values
Check the autopilot's circuit breaker for intermittent trip
Verify the ADC's power supply voltage with a multimeter
practice.explanation: With good wiring, a databus analyzer inserted between the ADC and autopilot will confirm whether the ADC is actually transmitting the altitude label at the expected update rate and with valid data — the direct test for this type of fault.

preassessment.question: What does a digital databus analyzer do?
choices: Measures LRU power supply voltage
Programs LRU configurations
Captures and decodes bus traffic to diagnose communication faults
Measures termination resistance

questions.learningObjective: Databus analyzer is most useful for capturing intermittent digital bus failures
questions.fullObjective: State that a digital databus analyzer or protocol analyzer is the test instrument most useful for capturing and evaluating intermittent digital databus failures.

flashcard.definition: A digital databus analyzer (protocol analyzer) is the best tool for capturing intermittent bus failures — it monitors continuously and time-stamps all bus activity, capturing the failure event when it occurs even if it is brief and transient.
flashcard.analogy: An intermittent bus failure is like a car noise that only happens sometimes. A databus analyzer is the dashcam that records everything — when the noise finally happens, you have proof of what it was.

jeopardy.question: For capturing and evaluating an intermittent digital databus failure that occurs randomly during flight, this instrument is the most useful because it monitors continuously and records all bus events.
choices: Protocol (databus) analyzer
Digital multimeter
Oscilloscope
Pitot-static tester
jeopardy.explanation: A protocol/databus analyzer captures all bus traffic continuously, recording error events and their timestamps even for brief, intermittent failures — unlike an oscilloscope which requires triggering at the right moment.

multipleChoice.question: Which instrument is most useful for diagnosing an intermittent ARINC 429 bus failure that occurs unpredictably?
choices: A digital multimeter measuring bus voltage
A digital databus (protocol) analyzer that continuously monitors and records all bus activity
An oscilloscope triggered on bus signal transitions
A megohmmeter measuring cable insulation resistance
multipleChoice.explanation: An intermittent failure by definition doesn't occur consistently. A databus analyzer continuously monitors and logs all bus activity, capturing the failure event with timestamp and context when it finally occurs.

practice.question: A GPS intermittently stops providing data to the EFIS, but always works on the ground. No faults are found on ground test. What instrument should be left installed to capture the next inflight failure?
choices: A multimeter with a peak-hold function on the bus voltage circuit
An oscilloscope triggered on GPS output signal transitions
A databus analyzer set to continuous record, capturing all ARINC 429 traffic between the GPS and EFIS to log the exact failure event when it occurs in flight
A BITE interrogator connected to the GPS maintenance port
practice.explanation: A databus analyzer in continuous record mode will capture the exact state of the bus when the failure occurs in flight — showing whether the GPS stops transmitting, sends invalid data, or the bus fails — providing definitive diagnostic information.

preassessment.question: Which tool is best for capturing an intermittent digital databus failure?
choices: DMM
Frequency counter
Oscilloscope
Protocol/databus analyzer

questions.learningObjective: Databus analyzer captures real-time bus traffic and decodes data words
questions.fullObjective: State the primary purpose of a digital databus analyzer in avionics troubleshooting: to capture bus traffic in real time, decode data words, and identify incorrect or missing transmissions.

flashcard.definition: A databus analyzer's primary troubleshooting purpose is to capture bus traffic in real time, decode individual data words (revealing label, value, and error status), and identify which transmissions are incorrect, corrupted, or missing entirely.
flashcard.analogy: Think of a databus analyzer like a network packet sniffer for avionics — it sees every data 'packet' (word) on the bus, decodes it from raw bits into readable labels and values, and flags anything that's wrong.

jeopardy.question: In avionics troubleshooting, this instrument captures real-time bus traffic, decodes data words to reveal their labels and values, and identifies transmissions that are incorrect or missing.
choices: BITE interrogator
Digital databus analyzer
Multimeter
Oscilloscope
jeopardy.explanation: A databus analyzer captures and decodes bus traffic in real time — showing each data word's label, value, and any error status — enabling direct identification of bad or missing transmissions.

multipleChoice.question: In avionics troubleshooting, what is the primary purpose of a digital databus analyzer?
choices: To measure the AC impedance of the bus cable at various frequencies
To program replacement LRUs with the correct ARINC label configuration
To capture bus traffic in real time, decode data words, and identify incorrect or missing transmissions
To test bus cable shielding effectiveness against electromagnetic interference
multipleChoice.explanation: The databus analyzer is the window into what's actually happening on the bus: real-time capture, word-by-word decoding, and automatic flagging of errors or missing labels.

practice.question: A flight computer shows a 'GPS invalid' flag even though the GPS appears operational. A databus analyzer is connected to the ARINC 429 output of the GPS. What information should the technician look for?
choices: Whether the GPS is drawing the correct current from its power supply bus
Whether the flight computer's BITE code for GPS is current or historical
Whether the GPS antenna cable impedance matches the specified 50-ohm characteristic
Whether the GPS is transmitting the expected ARINC 429 labels (position, integrity, status) with valid values and at the expected update rate
practice.explanation: The databus analyzer shows exactly what the GPS is transmitting: label presence, data values, and update rates. If the GPS is missing required labels or sending invalid status words, the analyzer will reveal this directly.

preassessment.question: What does a databus analyzer identify during troubleshooting?
choices: Incorrect or missing data transmissions
Cable impedance mismatches
LRU power consumption
Antenna radiation patterns

questions.learningObjective: Databus/protocol analyzer is the tool for digital databus traffic analysis
questions.fullObjective: State that a databus analyzer (protocol analyzer) is the tool commonly used to capture and analyze digital databus traffic in avionics maintenance.

flashcard.definition: A databus analyzer (also called a protocol analyzer) is the standard tool in avionics maintenance for capturing and analyzing digital databus traffic — available for ARINC 429, MIL-STD-1553, RS-232/485, and other protocols.
flashcard.analogy: Just as a mechanic has a scan tool for car computer diagnostics, an avionics technician has a protocol analyzer for databus diagnostics — the right specialized tool for the job.

jeopardy.question: The standard specialized test instrument used in avionics maintenance to capture and analyze ARINC 429 and other digital databus traffic is called by this name.
choices: Pitot-static tester
VSWR meter
Protocol analyzer (databus analyzer)
Logic probe
jeopardy.explanation: A protocol analyzer (databus analyzer) is the specialized instrument for digital bus traffic capture and analysis in avionics — connecting non-intrusively to the bus and decoding all protocol data.

multipleChoice.question: What instrument is commonly used in avionics maintenance to capture and analyze digital databus traffic?
choices: Digital multimeter — the most versatile test instrument for all avionics measurements
Spectrum analyzer — for analyzing the frequency content of digital signals
Oscilloscope — the standard instrument for all digital signal analysis
Databus analyzer (protocol analyzer) — specialized for capturing and decoding digital bus communication
multipleChoice.explanation: A databus analyzer (protocol analyzer) is the specialized tool for ARINC 429, MIL-STD-1553, and other avionics bus analysis. Oscilloscopes show waveforms but don't decode protocol data; DMMs show only voltage.

practice.question: A trainee asks which test instrument they will use most often for ARINC 429 troubleshooting. What is the correct answer?
choices: A databus (protocol) analyzer — it captures and decodes ARINC 429 traffic at the protocol level, showing labels, values, and errors
An oscilloscope — it shows the electrical signal on the bus in the most detail
A digital multimeter — it can measure bus voltage levels to diagnose most ARINC 429 problems
A time-domain reflectometer — it locates cable faults on the bus wire pair
practice.explanation: A databus analyzer is the primary tool for ARINC 429 work. While oscilloscopes and multimeters have roles, the analyzer is the only tool that decodes the protocol layer and shows what data is being communicated.

preassessment.question: What tool captures and analyzes digital databus traffic in avionics maintenance?
choices: Multimeter
Protocol (databus) analyzer
Oscilloscope
Spectrum analyzer

questions.learningObjective: Continuity test with multimeter confirms broken wire using resistance measurement
questions.fullObjective: Select a continuity test with a multimeter as the correct method to confirm whether a broken wire is causing a warning light to fail, by measuring resistance between the wire ends.

flashcard.definition: A continuity test uses a multimeter (typically in the ohms or continuity beep mode) to measure resistance between two ends of a conductor; near-zero resistance confirms a complete circuit, while 'OL' or infinite resistance confirms an open/broken wire.
flashcard.analogy: A continuity test is like testing whether a garden hose leaks by blowing through it — if air comes out the other end (near-zero resistance), the path is complete. If nothing comes out (OL), there's a break.

jeopardy.question: To confirm that a broken wire is preventing a warning light from illuminating, a technician should perform this type of test with a multimeter, measuring resistance between the wire's endpoints.
choices: Voltage measurement with the circuit energized
Insulation resistance test with a megohmmeter
Current measurement in series with the warning light
Continuity test measuring resistance between wire ends
jeopardy.explanation: A continuity test measures resistance between the two ends of a suspected broken wire. Near-zero ohms confirms an intact path; OL (infinite resistance) confirms the wire is broken.

multipleChoice.question: A warning light is not illuminating. The lamp tests good. What test should the technician use to determine if a broken wire is the cause?
choices: Perform a continuity test with a multimeter measuring resistance between the two ends of the suspect wire
Measure the voltage drop across the lamp with the circuit energized
Use an oscilloscope to check the lamp's activation signal waveform
Measure the current in the lamp circuit with an ammeter
multipleChoice.explanation: A continuity test directly tests wire integrity. With power OFF, measure resistance between wire ends: near-zero ohms = intact; OL = broken. This is the definitive test for a broken wire.

practice.question: After replacing a gear position indicator lamp, the light still does not illuminate when gear is selected down. The circuit breaker is OK. What should the technician test next?
choices: Replace the gear position switch — it is most likely faulty
Use a multimeter in continuity mode to test the wire from the switch to the indicator light for an open circuit
Test the power supply bus voltage at the main avionics bus bar
Check the landing gear mechanical position with a mirror and flashlight
practice.explanation: With the lamp confirmed good and the breaker OK, a broken wire is the next likely cause. A continuity test on the circuit wiring (power OFF) will confirm or eliminate this as the fault.

preassessment.question: What test confirms whether a wire is broken or intact?
choices: Voltage measurement
Current measurement
Continuity test with multimeter
Insulation resistance test

questions.learningObjective: Micrometer use: clean, zero, apply gentle consistent pressure
questions.fullObjective: Apply the correct procedure for accurate micrometer measurement: clean the measuring surfaces, zero the micrometer before use, and apply consistent gentle pressure when taking readings.

flashcard.definition: For accurate micrometer measurements: (1) clean both measuring surfaces (spindle and anvil) to remove debris, (2) zero the micrometer (close on nothing — verify it reads zero), (3) apply gentle, consistent pressure using the thimble ratchet (if equipped) — excessive pressure deforms the workpiece.
flashcard.analogy: Using a micrometer properly is like calibrating a scale: clean the pan (clean surfaces), check it reads zero with nothing on it (zero check), then place items with the same gentle touch each time (consistent pressure).

jeopardy.question: The three steps for accurate micrometer measurement are: clean the measuring surfaces, apply consistent gentle pressure using the ratchet thimble, and do this before taking any reading.
choices: Zero the micrometer with the surfaces closed
Lubricate the spindle thread
Set the reference temperature to 20°C
Mark the measurement point with a scribe
jeopardy.explanation: Zeroing the micrometer before use ensures the zero reference is correct. A micrometer that doesn't read zero when closed will have a constant offset error in every measurement.

multipleChoice.question: What are the correct steps for taking an accurate micrometer measurement?
choices: Lubricate the spindle, set to expected range, take three rapid readings and average
Clean the measuring surfaces, zero the micrometer before use, and apply consistent gentle pressure when taking the reading
Apply oil to the workpiece, close the micrometer firmly, and read the display
Lock the barrel before closing on the workpiece to prevent overshooting
multipleChoice.explanation: Micrometer accuracy requires: clean surfaces (no contamination affecting the gap), verified zero (no baseline offset), and consistent gentle pressure (not deforming the workpiece or getting different readings based on force).

practice.question: A technician is measuring a wire crimp cross-section with a micrometer. After taking the measurement, the result seems larger than expected. What should the technician verify first?
choices: That the micrometer anvil is the correct size for the wire gauge
Whether the wire is at the correct temperature for the measurement
Whether the micrometer was zeroed before use and the measuring faces were clean — a baseline error or debris between the faces would give a falsely large reading
Whether the measurement should be taken in metric rather than imperial units
practice.explanation: A larger-than-expected reading is consistent with either a zero-offset error (micrometer not zeroed) or debris on the measuring surfaces adding to the measured dimension. Check both before repeating the measurement.

preassessment.question: What must be done before taking a micrometer measurement?
choices: Lubricate spindle thread
Heat-stabilize the instrument
Set to expected range
Clean faces and zero the micrometer

questions.learningObjective: Screwdriver must fully engage the screw head to prevent cam-out
questions.fullObjective: Select a properly sized screwdriver that fully engages the screw head as the most important factor when using a manual screwdriver to prevent cam-out and screw head damage.

flashcard.definition: Cam-out occurs when a screwdriver slips out of the screw head under torque, damaging the head and potentially the surrounding surface. Using a properly sized screwdriver that fully fills and engages the screw head recess is the primary prevention — an undersized blade has less contact area and cams out more easily.
flashcard.analogy: Using a screwdriver that's too small is like trying to turn a deadbolt with a toothpick — the toothpick will snap out under pressure because it doesn't fully engage the mechanism. A proper key fills the slot completely.

jeopardy.question: The most important factor in preventing cam-out and screw head damage when using a manual screwdriver is using one that completely fills and does this to the screw head recess.
choices: Exceeds the torque specification of the fastener
Fully engages (fits snugly into) the screw head
Has a rubber-grip handle for maximum torque
Matches the color code of the screw head material
jeopardy.explanation: Full engagement means the blade or bit fills the screw head recess completely — maximizing contact area, distributing force evenly, and preventing the bit from riding up and camming out.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most important factor when selecting a manual screwdriver to prevent cam-out?
choices: The screwdriver should be as long as possible to provide maximum torque leverage
The screwdriver should be harder than the screw material to prevent wear
The screwdriver blade or bit should be properly sized to fully engage the screw head
The screwdriver should be rated for maximum torque exceeding the fastener specification
multipleChoice.explanation: Full engagement is the primary cam-out prevention measure. A correctly sized bit/blade fills the recess completely, maximizing the contact area that resists the tendency to cam out under torque.

practice.question: A technician uses a Phillips screwdriver that is one size too small to remove a panel screw and notices the bit slipping out of the head. What is the correct action?
choices: Apply more downward pressure and try again with the same bit
Add locking compound to the driver tip to prevent slipping
Use a flathead screwdriver in one of the Phillips slots for better contact
Select a correctly sized Phillips bit that fully engages the screw head recess — using the undersized bit risks damaging the screw head to the point of non-removal
practice.explanation: An undersized bit doesn't fill the Phillips recess — it contacts only the tips of the cross slots and cams out under torque. Using the correct size is the only proper fix.

preassessment.question: What prevents screwdriver cam-out?
choices: Proper size that fully engages the screw head
Maximum torque application
Rubber-grip handle
Hardened bit material

questions.learningObjective: Multimeter measures voltage, current, and resistance
questions.fullObjective: Explain the primary function of a multimeter as measuring voltage, current, and resistance in electrical circuits using a single instrument.

flashcard.definition: A multimeter (digital or analog) is a single instrument that measures multiple electrical quantities: voltage (volts AC and DC), current (amps AC and DC), and resistance (ohms) — replacing separate voltmeter, ammeter, and ohmmeter instruments.
flashcard.analogy: A multimeter is the Swiss Army knife of electrical test equipment — voltage meter, current meter, and ohmmeter all in one instrument, with a selector switch to choose which function you need.

jeopardy.question: A multimeter measures these three electrical quantities in circuits, combining what were traditionally three separate instruments into one.
choices: Frequency, power, and impedance
Capacitance, inductance, and impedance
Voltage, current, and resistance
Temperature, humidity, and pressure
jeopardy.explanation: A multimeter combines voltmeter (voltage), ammeter (current), and ohmmeter (resistance) functions in one instrument — hence 'multi' (multiple) meter.

multipleChoice.question: What are the three primary electrical quantities measured by a digital multimeter?
choices: Frequency, power factor, and efficiency
Capacitance, inductance, and RF output
Impedance, reactance, and phase angle
Voltage, current, and resistance
multipleChoice.explanation: A multimeter's three primary measurements are voltage (V), current (A), and resistance (Ω). Modern DMMs often add capacitance, frequency, temperature, and diode test, but the three core functions are V, A, and Ω.

practice.question: A new avionics apprentice is told to use a multimeter to diagnose an inoperative circuit. Which three basic measurements should they know how to take?
choices: Voltage across components, current in series with the circuit, and resistance of wire segments
Frequency, waveform, and phase
Capacitance, inductance, and impedance
Signal-to-noise ratio, bit error rate, and frequency response
practice.explanation: The three essential multimeter measurements for circuit diagnosis: voltage (identify where voltage is present/absent), current (confirm correct current flow), and resistance (check continuity and component values).

preassessment.question: What three quantities does a multimeter measure?
choices: Frequency, power, and impedance
Voltage, current, and resistance
Capacitance, inductance, and phase
Temperature, humidity, and pressure

questions.objective: State that Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for shop chemicals must be kept in an accessible location readily available to all personnel who work with or near hazardous materials.

flashcard.definition: Safety Data Sheets for shop chemicals must be kept in an accessible location readily available to ALL personnel who work with or near hazardous materials.
flashcard.analogy: Like a first-aid kit that must be visible and reachable by anyone who might need it — not locked away where only supervisors can access it.

jeopardy.clue: Federal regulations require these documents, which detail chemical hazards and emergency procedures, to be stored where every shop worker can reach them without delay.
choices: Safety Data Sheets (SDS)
The aircraft maintenance manual
The shop's insurance policy
OSHA 300 injury logs

multipleChoice.stem: Where must Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for shop chemicals be stored?
choices: In the supervisor's office for security
In an accessible location available to all personnel working with or near hazardous materials
In a locked cabinet accessible only to certified technicians
Digitally only, on the company intranet
multipleChoice.explanation: SDS must be readily accessible to ALL personnel who may be exposed — not restricted to supervisors or specific roles.

practice.stem: A new shop technician asks where the SDS binder is kept. The supervisor says it is locked in the office. Is this compliant?
choices: Yes, locking it prevents unauthorized chemical use
Yes, as long as the supervisor is always present
No — SDS must be accessible to all personnel without barriers
No — SDS must be posted on every wall of the shop
practice.explanation: SDS must be immediately accessible to everyone who works with or near the hazardous materials — locking them away violates the requirement.

preassessment.stem: Who must be able to access Safety Data Sheets in a repair shop?
choices: Supervisors only
Certified technicians only
Only the hazmat officer
All personnel working with or near hazardous materials

questions.objective: Describe proper disposal of hazardous materials in a GA repair environment as following EPA and local regulations, using licensed disposal vendors, and never pouring chemicals into drains or trash.

flashcard.definition: Hazardous materials must be disposed of by following EPA and local regulations, using licensed disposal vendors, and NEVER pouring chemicals down drains or into regular trash.
flashcard.analogy: Like recycling electronics — you can't toss a laptop in the garbage; it must go through a certified e-waste vendor.

jeopardy.clue: In a GA repair shop, disposing of this type of material requires a licensed vendor and must never involve dumping it down a drain or in the regular trash.
choices: Scrap aluminum shavings
Hazardous chemical waste
Used paper towels
Worn safety gloves (non-contaminated)

multipleChoice.stem: Which method is acceptable for disposing of used solvent in a GA avionics shop?
choices: Pour it down the utility sink drain
Absorb it and place in regular trash
Use a licensed hazardous waste disposal vendor per EPA and local regulations
Evaporate it outside away from the building
multipleChoice.explanation: Hazardous chemicals must go through licensed disposal vendors following EPA and local regulations — drains and regular trash are prohibited.

practice.stem: A technician finishes a cleaning job and has leftover solvent. What is the correct disposal procedure?
choices: Pour it down the floor drain — it is diluted enough
Dump it in the dumpster in a sealed bag
Leave it in an open container to evaporate
Follow EPA and local regulations using a licensed hazmat disposal vendor
practice.explanation: Leftover solvents are hazardous waste and must be handled through licensed vendors per regulatory requirements — never discarded in drains or trash.

preassessment.stem: What is prohibited when disposing of shop chemicals?
choices: Pouring chemicals down drains or in trash
Following EPA regulations
Using a licensed vendor
Labeling waste containers

questions.objective: Define a near-miss in a shop safety context as an unplanned event that did not result in injury or damage but had the potential to do so, and state that near-misses must be reported and investigated.

flashcard.definition: An unplanned event that did NOT result in injury or damage but had the POTENTIAL to do so. Near-misses MUST be reported and investigated.
flashcard.analogy: A car that nearly runs a red light without hitting anything — the near-miss reveals a hazard that needs to be corrected before the next attempt isn't so lucky.

jeopardy.clue: An avionics tech drops a soldering iron that narrowly misses a coworker and lands harmlessly on the floor. This unplanned event with no injury but real danger potential has a specific safety term.
choices: A tool audit failure
An acceptable risk
A near-miss
An OSHA recordable event

multipleChoice.stem: A technician slips on a wet floor but catches himself on a bench without falling. How should this event be classified and handled?
choices: It can be ignored — no one was hurt
It is an OSHA recordable incident requiring workers' compensation filing
It is only reportable if the technician required medical attention
It is a near-miss and must be reported and investigated
multipleChoice.explanation: A near-miss is any unplanned event with potential for injury or damage, even if none occurred. Near-misses must be reported and investigated to prevent recurrence.

practice.stem: During a bench job a tool rolls off the table, narrowly missing a passing coworker. No one is injured. What is the required action?
choices: Report and investigate it as a near-miss
Mention it informally to a coworker
No action needed — no one was hurt
File an OSHA 300 recordable injury report
practice.explanation: Near-misses with potential for injury must be reported and investigated regardless of outcome — this is how shops identify and eliminate hazards before an actual injury occurs.

preassessment.stem: What defines a near-miss in a shop safety context?
choices: Any event that causes minor injury
An unplanned event with no injury but potential for harm
A safety drill that fails inspection
An accident involving equipment damage only

questions.objective: State the first action when a burning insulation odor is detected in an avionics rack: remove aircraft power immediately and identify the source before any further troubleshooting.

flashcard.definition: When a burning insulation odor is detected in an avionics rack: FIRST remove aircraft power immediately, then identify the source before any further troubleshooting.
flashcard.analogy: Like smelling smoke in a building — the first response is to evacuate (remove the hazard energy), not investigate while the danger is still present.

jeopardy.clue: This is the first required action when a tech detects a burning electrical odor from an avionics rack during a bench test.
choices: Continue testing to isolate the source
Grab a fire extinguisher
Call the fire department
Remove aircraft power immediately

multipleChoice.stem: A technician detects the smell of burning insulation while troubleshooting a powered avionics rack. What is the correct FIRST action?
choices: Remove aircraft power immediately, then identify the source
Spray electrical contact cleaner to cool the area
Continue the test to locate the fault before it resets
Use a thermal camera to scan the rack while powered
multipleChoice.explanation: Burning insulation indicates active electrical damage or fire risk. Power must be removed FIRST before any investigation — continuing to apply power risks fire or further wiring damage.

practice.stem: During an avionics bench test you smell burning plastic coming from the rack. The test was almost complete. What should you do?
choices: Quickly finish the test to capture the fault data first
Remove power immediately, then locate the source before proceeding
Grab a CO2 extinguisher and stand by while continuing the test
Pull only the suspected LRU circuit breaker and continue
practice.explanation: A burning smell indicates active danger. Removing power is the only safe first step — no test result justifies continuing to apply power when insulation may be burning.

preassessment.stem: What is the first action when burning insulation odor is detected in an avionics rack?
choices: Grab a fire extinguisher
Call maintenance control
Remove aircraft power immediately
Open the rack doors for ventilation

questions.objective: State the required preparation before transporting a spillable lead-acid battery: ensure caps are tight, the battery is fully charged, and it is secured in a non-conductive, leak-proof container.

flashcard.definition: Before transporting a spillable lead-acid battery: (1) ensure vent caps are tight, (2) battery is fully charged, and (3) secured in a non-conductive, leak-proof container.
flashcard.analogy: Like transporting a full pot of hot soup — seal the lid tight, make sure it's stable, and put it in a container that won't let anything leak out.

jeopardy.clue: These three steps — checking vent caps, confirming charge state, and using a non-conductive sealed container — must be completed before moving this type of aircraft battery.
choices: Lead-acid battery transport preparation
NiCd battery replacement procedure
Battery load-test readiness check
Lithium battery shipping compliance

multipleChoice.stem: Which set of precautions is required BEFORE transporting a spillable lead-acid aircraft battery?
choices: Discharge the battery, loosen the vent caps for pressure relief, and place in a cardboard box
Ensure caps are tight, battery is fully charged, and secured in a non-conductive leak-proof container
Only verify the charge level — no special container is needed for shop transport
Wrap terminals with tape and carry the battery by its handle
multipleChoice.explanation: Lead-acid batteries contain sulfuric acid. Tight caps prevent spills, the non-conductive leak-proof container protects against acid contact, and a proper charge state reduces gassing.

practice.stem: A tech needs to move a spillable lead-acid battery across the shop. Which preparation is correct?
choices: Put it in a plastic bag — that is sufficient containment
Check that vent caps are tight and carry it carefully by hand
Verify caps are tight, battery is charged, and secure it in a non-conductive, leak-proof container
Discharge it first to reduce pressure, then transport in any container
practice.explanation: All three steps are required: tight caps, proper charge state, and a non-conductive leak-proof container — each addresses a specific spillage or hazard risk.

preassessment.stem: What container is required when transporting a spillable lead-acid battery?
choices: A conductive metal tray
Any sealed plastic box
A cardboard box with absorbent material
A non-conductive, leak-proof container

questions.objective: Apply the critical safety precaution when using a megohmmeter: ensure the circuit under test is completely de-energized and all capacitors are discharged before connecting the meter.

flashcard.definition: Before connecting a megohmmeter: (1) completely de-energize the circuit, and (2) discharge ALL capacitors. The megohmmeter applies high voltage — residual charge or live circuits can cause equipment damage or injury.
flashcard.analogy: Like working on a camera flash capacitor — even after removing the battery, stored charge remains and can shock you. You must always discharge first.

jeopardy.clue: Before connecting this high-voltage insulation resistance test instrument, two safety steps are mandatory: verify the circuit is de-energized and discharge all capacitors.
choices: Oscilloscope
Megohmmeter
Milliammeter
LCR meter

multipleChoice.stem: What is the critical safety precaution before connecting a megohmmeter to a circuit?
choices: Set the meter to AC mode first, then connect
Verify power is off, but capacitor discharge is optional for low-voltage circuits
Ensure the circuit is completely de-energized and all capacitors are discharged
Keep power applied at low voltage to verify meter connection
multipleChoice.explanation: Megohmmeters apply high test voltages (500–5000 V). If the circuit is live or capacitors hold charge, connecting the meter risks equipment damage or technician injury.

practice.stem: Before performing an insulation resistance test with a megohmmeter on an avionics bus wiring bundle, what must be done FIRST?
choices: Remove only the avionics LRUs — bus wiring can stay powered
Set the megohmmeter range and connect while power is reduced to idle
Only discharge capacitors — a live circuit improves test accuracy
Completely de-energize the aircraft and discharge all capacitors in the circuit
practice.explanation: Both steps are mandatory: full de-energization AND capacitor discharge. A megohmmeter applies high voltage and any residual energy can cause damage or injury.

preassessment.stem: What must be done before connecting a megohmmeter?
choices: De-energize the circuit and discharge all capacitors
Connect to ground first
Set the range to the highest voltage
Verify meter battery level

questions.objective: State that the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the document containing specific safety information about a chemical solvent, including hazards, handling procedures, and first-aid measures.

flashcard.definition: The document containing specific safety information about a chemical, including hazards, handling procedures, first-aid measures, and emergency response — required for every hazardous chemical in the shop.
flashcard.analogy: The SDS is the chemical's complete 'owner's manual and warning label' — it tells you exactly what you're dealing with and what to do if something goes wrong.

jeopardy.clue: This document, required for every hazardous chemical in the shop, lists the substance's hazards, safe handling procedures, required PPE, and first-aid steps for exposure.
choices: Environmental Impact Assessment
Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM)
Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
Material Certification (C of C)

multipleChoice.stem: A technician needs to know the health hazards and first-aid steps for a chemical solvent. Which document should they consult?
choices: The product label only — it contains all required information
The shop's tool inventory log
The OSHA 300 injury log
The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for that chemical
multipleChoice.explanation: The SDS contains comprehensive safety information for each chemical: hazards, handling procedures, PPE requirements, and first-aid measures — the product label alone is insufficient.

practice.stem: A tech splashes a cleaning solvent on their skin. Where do they find the correct first-aid procedure?
choices: The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for that solvent
Ask the supervisor — no single reference document exists
The aircraft maintenance manual
The shop's OSHA 300 injury log
practice.explanation: The SDS for each chemical includes specific first-aid measures for skin contact, inhalation, ingestion, and eye exposure — it is the primary safety reference for any chemical exposure event.

preassessment.stem: What document contains the hazards, handling, and first-aid information for a shop chemical?
choices: Material Certification
Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
Maintenance Manual
OSHA 300 Log

questions.objective: Describe safe practice around moving rotorcraft: stay clear of the tail rotor, approach only from the front within the pilot's field of view, and never approach until the pilot signals it is safe.

flashcard.definition: Around a running helicopter: (1) stay clear of the tail rotor at all times, (2) approach only from the front within the pilot's field of view, (3) never approach until the pilot signals it is safe.
flashcard.analogy: Like approaching a running industrial fan — you never walk around the back where you can't see the blades clearly, and you always wait for someone to signal it's safe.

jeopardy.clue: These three ground rules — avoid the rear, approach from the front, and wait for the pilot's signal — define the correct way to approach a running helicopter on the ramp.
choices: Engine run-up clearance protocol
Pushback crew coordination procedure
Rotor blade folding checklist
Rotorcraft approach safety rules

multipleChoice.stem: When approaching a running helicopter on the ramp, what is the correct practice?
choices: Stay clear of the tail rotor, approach from the front, and wait for the pilot's signal
Approach from the rear where ground wash is least, staying low
Approach from either side — the main rotor is the only real hazard
Approach only after the engine is at idle power regardless of pilot signal
multipleChoice.explanation: The tail rotor is nearly invisible when spinning and is a lethal hazard. You must approach from the front where the pilot can see you, and only after receiving the go-ahead signal.

practice.stem: A technician needs to approach a helicopter that has just landed with rotors still turning. What is the correct course of action?
choices: Move quickly toward the cargo door from the rear to avoid the main rotor
Wait at a safe distance, approach from the front within the pilot's field of view only after the pilot signals
Approach from either side once you confirm the main rotor is slowing
Approach as soon as the engine spools down, regardless of rotor speed
practice.explanation: Never approach from the rear (tail rotor hazard), never approach without the pilot's signal, and always approach from the front so the pilot sees you and can wave you off if unsafe.

preassessment.stem: From which direction should you approach a running helicopter?
choices: From the rear to avoid the main rotor downwash
From either side
From the front within the pilot's field of view
From whichever side the wind is coming from

questions.objective: Describe the recommended action for a hydraulic fluid spill near the avionics bench: contain the spill immediately using absorbent materials, remove power from nearby equipment, and clean up per the shop's hazmat procedures.

flashcard.definition: For a hydraulic fluid spill near avionics equipment: (1) contain immediately with absorbent materials, (2) remove power from nearby equipment, (3) clean up following shop hazmat procedures.
flashcard.analogy: Like a kitchen grease fire — contain it first, cut the ignition source (power), then clean up properly.

jeopardy.clue: These three steps — containing with absorbent materials, cutting power to nearby electronics, and following hazmat cleanup procedures — define the correct response to this type of bench-area contamination event.
choices: Hydraulic fluid spill near avionics equipment
An ESD event at the bench
A battery electrolyte spill on the tarmac
A fuel overflow during gravity fill

multipleChoice.stem: Hydraulic fluid spills onto the avionics bench area. What is the correct immediate response?
choices: Wipe it up quickly with shop rags and continue work
Contain the spill with absorbent materials, remove power from nearby equipment, then clean up per hazmat procedures
Dilute the spill with water to reduce fire risk before cleaning
Only clean up after the current job is completed to avoid work interruption
multipleChoice.explanation: Hydraulic fluid is flammable and corrosive. You must contain it first, remove electrical power (ignition hazard), then follow established hazmat cleanup procedures.

practice.stem: During a hydraulic line R&R near the avionics bay, fluid drips onto the bench where a powered unit is being tested. What should the technician do first?
choices: Finish the current test cycle, then clean up
Wipe the bench with a dry rag while the unit stays powered
Contain the spill with absorbent materials and remove power from nearby equipment
Move the powered unit to another bench and leave the spill for the night crew
practice.explanation: Hydraulic fluid near energized equipment creates a fire hazard. The spill must be contained and power removed before any cleanup or further work.

preassessment.stem: What is the first action for a hydraulic fluid spill near powered avionics equipment?
choices: Dilute with water
Continue the job to avoid losing test data
Call the fire marshal before touching anything
Contain the spill with absorbent materials and remove power

questions.objective: Describe the accepted procedure for disposing of a Nickel-Cadmium battery: follow local hazardous materials disposal regulations and do not discard in regular trash.

flashcard.definition: Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) batteries must be disposed of by following local hazardous materials regulations — they CANNOT be discarded in regular trash because cadmium is a toxic heavy metal.
flashcard.analogy: Like old paint — you can't toss it in the garbage. It must go through an approved hazmat collection event or licensed disposal service.

jeopardy.clue: Cadmium's toxicity means these rechargeable aircraft batteries cannot be thrown in regular trash and must be handled through hazardous materials disposal channels.
choices: Sealed lead-acid batteries
Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) batteries
Alkaline AA batteries
Lithium coin cells

multipleChoice.stem: An aircraft NiCd battery has reached end-of-life. What is the correct disposal procedure?
choices: Discharge fully and place in regular trash — it is inert when empty
Puncture the cells for safe venting before disposal
Follow local hazardous materials disposal regulations — do not put in regular trash
Return it to the original manufacturer only — no other method is allowed
multipleChoice.explanation: Cadmium is a toxic heavy metal — NiCd batteries must go through regulated hazmat disposal channels regardless of state of charge. Regular trash disposal is prohibited.

practice.stem: A technician has a condemned aircraft NiCd battery ready for disposal. A coworker says to toss it in the dumpster. What is the correct response?
choices: Follow the coworker's advice — it's just a battery
Drain the electrolyte first, then dispose in trash
Send it back to the aircraft manufacturer for credit
Refuse — NiCd batteries must be disposed of per local hazmat regulations, not in regular trash
practice.explanation: NiCd batteries contain cadmium, a regulated hazardous material. Disposing in regular trash is illegal and harmful — licensed hazmat disposal is required.

preassessment.stem: Why can't a NiCd aircraft battery be discarded in regular trash?
choices: Cadmium is a toxic heavy metal requiring regulated disposal
They retain voltage that can start fires
They are too heavy for standard waste bins
Federal law requires all batteries be returned to the OEM

questions.objective: Describe how to check a fire extinguisher's readiness in a GA avionics shop: verify the pressure gauge is in the green zone, inspect the safety pin and tamper seal, check for visible damage, and confirm the annual inspection tag is current.

flashcard.definition: To verify a fire extinguisher is ready: (1) pressure gauge in green zone, (2) safety pin intact with tamper seal, (3) no visible damage to body or hose, (4) annual inspection tag current.
flashcard.analogy: Like a pre-flight walkaround — you check each item methodically so that when you need it, it works.

jeopardy.clue: Verifying this shop safety device involves checking the pressure gauge, confirming the pin and seal are intact, inspecting for damage, and ensuring the annual tag is current.
choices: Emergency shower flow check
Ground fault circuit interrupter test
Fire extinguisher readiness inspection
ESD wrist strap continuity test

multipleChoice.stem: Which combination of checks confirms a fire extinguisher is ready for service in a GA avionics shop?
choices: Verify the nozzle is unobstructed and the gauge reads any positive pressure
Weigh it to confirm charge weight only
Shake it to verify agent is loose inside
Pressure gauge in green, safety pin intact, no damage, annual tag current
multipleChoice.explanation: All four checks are required: gauge in green zone, intact pin/seal, no physical damage, and a current annual inspection tag — all must pass for the extinguisher to be considered serviceable.

practice.stem: You are doing a monthly shop safety walk. Which checks confirm the fire extinguishers are serviceable?
choices: Pressure gauge in green, safety pin and tamper seal intact, no visible damage, annual tag current
Gauge is in the green AND the annual inspection tag is current — no other checks needed
Only the annual tag matters — manufacturers certify the rest
Test-discharge a small amount to confirm agent is still good
practice.explanation: A complete serviceability check requires all four elements. A partial check (gauge only or tag only) may miss a cracked hose, pulled pin, or expired certification.

preassessment.stem: What indicates a fire extinguisher needs to be taken out of service?
choices: The gauge is in the green zone
The pressure gauge is in the red zone
The safety pin is intact
The annual tag was signed last week

questions.objective: State the most important first step for a new technician handling solvents and chemicals: read the SDS for each chemical before use to understand its hazards, required PPE, and emergency procedures.

flashcard.definition: Before handling any solvent or chemical, a new technician MUST read the SDS for each chemical to understand its hazards, required PPE, and emergency procedures.
flashcard.analogy: Like reading an instruction manual before using a power tool — you need to know the dangers before you pick it up.

jeopardy.clue: Reading this document for each chemical before first use gives a new technician the hazards, PPE requirements, and emergency steps needed for safe handling.
choices: The equipment rental agreement
The shop's floor plan
The aircraft wiring diagram
The Safety Data Sheet (SDS)

multipleChoice.stem: A new avionics technician is about to use a chemical solvent for the first time. What is the MOST important first step?
choices: Read the SDS for the solvent to understand hazards, PPE, and emergency procedures
Open the solvent in a ventilated area and proceed
Ask a coworker how much solvent to use
Don latex gloves and a respirator as general precaution without reading the SDS
multipleChoice.explanation: Different chemicals have different hazards, PPE needs, and first-aid steps. Reading the SDS for EACH chemical before use ensures the technician uses the correct protective measures.

practice.stem: On their first day in the avionics shop, a technician is asked to clean a connector with a solvent they've never used. What should they do before opening the container?
choices: Use the same PPE they used with yesterday's solvent — most solvents are similar
Read the SDS for that specific solvent to learn its hazards, PPE, and emergency procedures
Ask the supervisor which PPE to wear
Apply the chemical outdoors to eliminate fume risks without needing the SDS
practice.explanation: Each chemical has its own SDS with specific hazard information. Using PPE guidance from a different chemical or relying on verbal instruction without reading the SDS is not compliant.

preassessment.stem: What must a new technician do before using a chemical solvent for the first time?
