choices: Sign the shop chemical inventory log
Test the solvent on a small area first
Read the SDS for that specific chemical
Check the expiration date on the container

questions.objective: Apply the correct first-aid response to an electrical shock victim: ensure the area is safe, disconnect the power source before touching the victim, then call for emergency assistance.

flashcard.definition: For an electrical shock victim: (1) FIRST ensure the area is safe, (2) disconnect the power source before touching the victim, (3) call for emergency assistance. Never touch a victim who may still be energized.
flashcard.analogy: Like a water rescue — you don't jump in and grab a drowning person who might pull you under. You neutralize the danger (throw a line, cut power) before making contact.

jeopardy.clue: The correct sequence for helping an electrical shock victim: verify the area is safe, cut power before touching the person, then summon emergency help — in that order.
choices: Electrical shock victim first-aid response
Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) algorithm
Chemical burn treatment protocol
Fainting response procedure

multipleChoice.stem: A coworker receives an electrical shock and is still in contact with the circuit. What is the correct FIRST action?
choices: Immediately pull them away from the circuit with your hands
Ensure the area is safe and disconnect power before touching the victim
Shout for help while grabbing the victim to provide support
Begin CPR immediately while a coworker calls 911
multipleChoice.explanation: Touching an energized victim can electrocute the rescuer. The first step is to ensure YOUR safety, then disconnect power — only then is it safe to touch the victim and call for emergency help.

practice.stem: You see a coworker collapse after contacting an aircraft power bus. Their hand is still resting near the circuit. What is your first action?
choices: Grab their shoulders and drag them away quickly
Start chest compressions — cardiac arrest is the most urgent concern
Ensure the area is safe and remove power before making any contact with the victim
Call 911 first, then approach the victim to assess breathing
practice.explanation: You must ensure the area is safe and power is disconnected BEFORE touching the victim. Touching an energized victim makes you a second victim — this step cannot be skipped.

preassessment.stem: What must happen before you touch an electrical shock victim?
choices: Verify they are breathing
Call 911
Apply a tourniquet
Disconnect the power source

questions.objective: State that flammable liquids must be stored in approved, labeled, self-closing safety containers in a dedicated flammable storage cabinet away from ignition sources in an avionics shop.

flashcard.definition: Flammable liquids must be stored in: (1) approved, labeled, self-closing safety containers, (2) inside a dedicated flammable storage cabinet, (3) away from all ignition sources.
flashcard.analogy: Like storing gunpowder — it needs its own isolated container in its own isolated cabinet, not sitting next to a spark source on an open shelf.

jeopardy.clue: These self-closing, approved, labeled containers kept in a dedicated cabinet away from sparks are the OSHA-compliant way to store volatile cleaning solvents in an avionics shop.
choices: Standard open-top drum containers
Flammable liquid safety containers
General-purpose chemical shelving units
Cardboard-lined solvent boxes

multipleChoice.stem: How must flammable liquids be stored in a GA avionics shop?
choices: In any sealed container on the general tool shelving
In open cans under the bench to allow vapors to dissipate
In approved, labeled, self-closing safety containers in a dedicated flammable storage cabinet away from ignition sources
In any cabinet as long as it is labeled 'Flammable'
multipleChoice.explanation: Flammable liquid storage requires three elements: approved self-closing containers, a dedicated flammable storage cabinet, and separation from ignition sources.

practice.stem: During a safety inspection, a technician finds MEK solvent in an unlabeled mason jar stored near a soldering station. What is the violation?
choices: The only issue is lack of labeling — the container itself is acceptable
Proximity to the soldering station is acceptable as long as the jar is sealed
Mason jars are acceptable for sealed storage of any non-corrosive solvent
The solvent must be in an approved, labeled, self-closing container in a flammable cabinet away from ignition sources
practice.explanation: Multiple violations exist: unapproved container, no label, not self-closing, and located next to an ignition source. All three storage requirements must be met simultaneously.

preassessment.stem: Where must flammable liquids be stored in an avionics shop?
choices: In a dedicated flammable storage cabinet away from ignition sources
In any sealed container in the parts room
On open shelving in the main work area
Under the bench in original shipping containers

questions.objective: State the acceptable twist rate for the double-twist safety wire method as 6 to 8 twists per inch for standard aviation safety wiring applications.

flashcard.definition: The acceptable twist rate for the double-twist safety wire method is 6 to 8 twists per inch for standard aviation safety wiring applications.
flashcard.analogy: Like tightening a coil spring — too few twists and it's loose and ineffective; too many and it kinks and weakens. The 6–8 range hits the sweet spot for tension and integrity.

jeopardy.clue: This specified range — 6 to 8 per inch — defines how tightly to apply the double-twist method when installing safety wire on aviation fasteners.
choices: The minimum thread engagement for AN nuts
The maximum torque tolerance for AN bolts
The acceptable twist rate for safety wire
The pitch count for aviation-grade hex bolts

multipleChoice.stem: What is the acceptable twist rate for double-twist safety wire on aviation fasteners?
choices: 3 to 5 twists per inch
As tight as possible — maximum twists are always preferred
10 to 12 twists per inch
6 to 8 twists per inch
multipleChoice.explanation: 6 to 8 twists per inch is the standard for double-twist safety wire. Too few twists (under 6) are loose and non-compliant; too many (over 8) may kink or weaken the wire.

practice.stem: An inspector reviews safety wire installation on an avionics rack connector. The tech twisted 10 turns per inch. Is this acceptable?
choices: No — the acceptable range is 6 to 8 twists per inch; 10 twists per inch is non-compliant
Yes — the limit is a minimum of 6, no maximum applies
Yes — more twists always means better retention
No — safety wire must be replaced with lockwire above 8 twists per inch
practice.explanation: The specified range is 6 to 8 twists per inch. 10 twists per inch exceeds the maximum and the installation is non-compliant — excessive twisting can kink or weaken the wire.

preassessment.stem: What is the double-twist safety wire twist rate for standard aviation applications?
choices: 2 to 4 per inch
6 to 8 per inch
4 to 6 per inch
8 to 10 per inch

questions.objective: Given a technician feeling dizzy from solvent fumes, determine the most correct response: immediately leave the area, move to fresh air, notify a supervisor, and report the incident per shop safety procedures before returning to work.

flashcard.definition: If a technician feels dizzy from solvent fumes: (1) leave the area immediately, (2) move to fresh air, (3) notify a supervisor, (4) report the incident per safety procedures — before returning to work.
flashcard.analogy: Like a scuba diver whose air runs low — surface immediately (leave the area), get to safety (fresh air), report to your dive master (supervisor), and log the incident before going back in.

jeopardy.clue: Leave the area, get fresh air, notify a supervisor, and file an incident report — these four steps define the correct response when a technician feels dizzy in this type of chemical exposure situation.
choices: Battery acid splash first aid
ESD wrist strap malfunction procedure
Confined space entry emergency exit
Solvent fume overexposure response

multipleChoice.stem: A technician begins to feel dizzy while using a cleaning solvent in the shop. What is the correct course of action?
choices: Immediately leave the area, move to fresh air, notify a supervisor, and report the incident before returning
Open a window, put on a respirator, and continue the job
Take a short break at the bench and let the dizziness pass before continuing
Only notify a supervisor if the dizziness persists for more than 30 minutes
multipleChoice.explanation: Dizziness from solvent fumes indicates overexposure to toxic vapors. Immediate removal from the area and fresh air is the first priority, followed by supervisor notification and incident reporting.

practice.stem: While cleaning avionics connectors with MEK in a partially ventilated area, a tech feels lightheaded. The job is nearly complete. What should the tech do?
choices: Finish the job quickly — the fumes will clear when you stop
Stop immediately, leave to fresh air, tell the supervisor, and report the incident before returning
Switch to a different solvent and continue
Put on a dust mask and complete the last few connectors
practice.explanation: No job completion timeline justifies continued exposure when symptoms appear. Leaving immediately protects health; reporting documents the incident and triggers ventilation improvement.

preassessment.stem: What is the first action when a technician feels dizzy from solvent fumes?
choices: Open a window
Put on a half-face respirator
Immediately leave the area and move to fresh air
Sit down and rest until the feeling passes

questions.objective: Select strict tool control — counting tools before and after each job and immediately cleaning up dropped hardware and debris — as the most effective practice for preventing FOD in an avionics shop.

flashcard.definition: The most effective FOD prevention practice is strict tool control: count tools BEFORE and AFTER every job, and immediately retrieve and clean up any dropped hardware or debris.
flashcard.analogy: Like a surgeon counting instruments before and after an operation — every item must be accounted for, because a missing one means something dangerous was left inside.

jeopardy.clue: Counting tools at the start and end of every job and immediately retrieving any dropped hardware defines this practice, which is the most effective way to prevent foreign object damage in avionics work.
choices: Strict tool control for FOD prevention
ESD wrist strap verification
Pre-installation torque logging
Wire bundle continuity checking

multipleChoice.stem: Which practice is MOST effective at preventing Foreign Object Damage (FOD) in an avionics shop?
choices: Using only snap-on toolboxes with latching lids
Strict tool control — counting tools before and after each job and immediately cleaning up dropped hardware
Performing tool inventory once per week
Restricting shop access to credentialed technicians only
multipleChoice.explanation: Tool control is the foundation of FOD prevention — counting tools before and after every job ensures nothing is left inside an aircraft, and immediate cleanup of dropped hardware prevents items from migrating into aircraft structure.

practice.stem: A technician drops a small screw near an open avionics bay. They cannot immediately find it. What is the most FOD-safe action?
choices: Note the loss and proceed — screws do not cause serious FOD
Cover the open bay and continue the job; retrieve the screw at the end of the shift
Stop work, find and retrieve the screw immediately before any panels are closed
Inform QC at the end of the day and let them do the FOD walk
practice.explanation: Dropped hardware must be retrieved IMMEDIATELY before work continues — allowing a missing fastener to migrate into aircraft structure is a serious FOD risk.

preassessment.stem: When must dropped hardware be retrieved in an avionics FOD prevention program?
choices: At the end of the shift
After the job is complete
Before the next maintenance check
Immediately, before continuing work

questions.objective: Apply the correct practice to prevent ESD damage to sensitive avionics: use a properly grounded anti-static wrist strap and handle components only at ESD-protected workstations.

flashcard.definition: To prevent ESD damage to avionics: (1) use a properly grounded anti-static wrist strap, and (2) handle components ONLY at ESD-protected workstations.
flashcard.analogy: Like handling photographic film in a darkroom — the environment must be specially controlled before you open the package, not after.

jeopardy.clue: A grounded anti-static wrist strap and a specially equipped handling station are the two required safeguards before touching any sensitive avionics component.
choices: High-voltage insulation test setup
ESD damage prevention requirements
Battery load-test bench configuration
Antenna RF exposure safety protocol

multipleChoice.stem: A technician needs to remove a CMOS processor board from an avionics LRU. Which protective practice is required?
choices: Wear latex gloves and hold the board by its edges
Work quickly to minimize exposure time to ambient static
Use a properly grounded anti-static wrist strap and work only at an ESD-protected station
Ground the LRU chassis only — the board does not need additional protection
multipleChoice.explanation: ESD damage can be caused by static discharge too small to feel. A grounded wrist strap and ESD-protected workstation are both required to safely handle CMOS and other static-sensitive components.

practice.stem: A technician picks up a static-sensitive LRU card at a standard metal bench without a wrist strap, believing metal benches are inherently grounded. Is this correct ESD practice?
choices: Yes — metal benches provide an adequate ground path
Yes — ESD protection is only needed for components rated below 5V
No — the technician should have worn two wrist straps for dual protection
No — a properly grounded anti-static wrist strap is required, and handling must occur at an ESD-protected station
practice.explanation: A standard metal bench is NOT an ESD-protected workstation. The technician must use a properly grounded wrist strap AND an ESD-protected station — both are required.

preassessment.stem: What two protections are required when handling ESD-sensitive avionics components?
choices: Grounded wrist strap and ESD-protected workstation
Latex gloves and a grounded bench
Anti-static bag and dry work environment
Rubber-soled boots and metal bench

questions.objective: Given a small screw dropped behind a panel during avionics installation, determine the correct action: stop work, retrieve the screw before closing any panels, and log it as a FOD prevention measure.

flashcard.definition: If a screw is dropped behind a panel during avionics installation: (1) STOP work, (2) retrieve the screw BEFORE closing any panels, (3) log it as a FOD prevention measure.
flashcard.analogy: Like a surgeon who drops an instrument inside a patient — you stop the procedure, retrieve the item, and document it. You don't close up and hope for the best.

jeopardy.clue: Stop work, find the item before closing the panels, and log the event — these are the required steps when this type of small fastener goes missing during avionics panel installation.
choices: Panel alignment torque verification
Tool audit discrepancy procedure
Dropped screw FOD prevention response
Safety wire installation protocol

multipleChoice.stem: During avionics installation, a screw drops behind a panel and is not immediately visible. What is the correct action?
choices: Close the panel and notify QC on the job card
Complete the installation and perform a final FOD walk after
Replace the missing screw with a spare and note the discrepancy on the work order
Stop work, retrieve the screw before closing any panels, and log the event
multipleChoice.explanation: No panel may be closed until the dropped hardware is retrieved. A missing screw inside an aircraft structure is a serious FOD hazard, and the event must be logged per FOD prevention procedures.

practice.stem: A tech drops a #8-32 screw while installing an avionics tray. It disappears into the floor structure. There are two more screws to install before the panel can be closed. What should the tech do?
choices: Stop work, locate and retrieve the dropped screw, then log the event before continuing
Close the panel using the remaining screws and flag it on the paperwork
Install the remaining two screws and retrieve the dropped one at the same time
Replace the dropped screw with a spare — the original screw is not traceable anyway
practice.explanation: Work stops until the dropped hardware is found. Closing panels before retrieval makes the screw a permanent FOD hazard inside the aircraft — this is not acceptable under any workload schedule.

preassessment.stem: A screw drops behind a panel during avionics installation. What must happen before any panels are closed?
choices: Note the loss on the work order
Retrieve the dropped screw
Replace it with a spare
Ask the inspector for a waiver

questions.learningObjective: Understand Test equipment calibration documentation
questions.fullObjective: Documenting that test equipment was within its calibration interval at the time of use proves the measurements are traceable to known standards.

jeopardy.question: After completing a 91.411 pitot-static test, the maintenance record must include the specific test equipment used — including this detail about each instrument, which proves the measurements were valid.
choices: The equipment's original purchase date and vendor
The calibration due date, confirming the equipment was in calibration at the time of the test
The equipment's serial number and firmware version
The equipment manufacturer's ISO 9001 certification number
jeopardy.explanation: Documenting that test equipment was within its calibration interval at the time of use proves the measurements are traceable to known standards.

The equipment's serial number and firmware version
The equipment manufacturer's ISO 9001 certification number
practice.explanation: Documenting that test equipment was within its calibration interval at the time of use proves the measurements are traceable to known standards.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Common §43.9 deficiency
questions.fullObjective: The technician's certificate number is the most commonly omitted §43.9 element.

jeopardy.question: A technician signs off an avionics installation in the aircraft logbook but omits this specific §43.9 element — which is the single most common regulatory deficiency found during FSDO surveillance of Part 145 shops.
choices: The aircraft tail number
The work order reference number
The technician's certificate number
The shop's repair station number
jeopardy.explanation: The technician's certificate number is the most commonly omitted §43.9 element.

multipleChoice.question: A technician signs off an avionics installation in the aircraft logbook but omits this specific §43.9 element — which is the single most common regulatory deficiency found during FSDO surveillance of Part 145 shops.

The technician's certificate number
The shop's repair station number

questions.learningObjective: Understand Maintenance record correction procedure
questions.fullObjective: Part 43 maintenance records cannot be erased, whited out, or removed. The correct procedure is a new separate entry referencing the original.

jeopardy.question: A technician discovers that a previous maintenance record entry references the wrong part number for an installed component. Under Part 43, the correct action is this — not simply crossing it out or adding a note.
choices: Make a new corrective entry referencing the original entry, with the correct part number and a new signature
White out the incorrect number and write the correct one with initials and date
Contact the FAA FSDO to request a formal records amendment
Remove the page from the logbook and replace it with a corrected version
jeopardy.explanation: Part 43 maintenance records cannot be erased, whited out, or removed. The correct procedure is a new separate entry referencing the original.

questions.learningObjective: Understand 91.411 pitot-static interval
questions.fullObjective: Both 91.411 (altimeter/pitot-static) and 91.413 (transponder) require testing every 24 calendar months.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR 91.411, altimeter and pitot-static system inspections must be performed at this fixed interval — the same interval required for transponder checks under 91.413.
choices: Every 12 calendar months
Every 100 flight hours
At each annual inspection
Every 24 calendar months
jeopardy.explanation: Both 91.411 (altimeter/pitot-static) and 91.413 (transponder) require testing every 24 calendar months.

multipleChoice.question: Per 14 CFR 91.411, altimeter and pitot-static system inspections must be performed at this fixed interval — the same interval required for transponder checks under 91.413.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Mode C altitude accuracy test
questions.fullObjective: Mode C altitude reporting accuracy must be verified within ±125 feet per Part 43 Appendix F.

jeopardy.question: During a transponder test per Part 43 Appendix F, the test set verifies reply frequency, power output, suppression, and this parameter — which confirms the transponder correctly reports the aircraft's barometric altitude.
choices: Mode C altitude encoding accuracy within ±125 feet
ATC radar interrogation response delay within 3 microseconds
Squawk code assignment timing within 10 milliseconds
Antenna VSWR below 1.5:1 at 1090 MHz
jeopardy.explanation: Mode C altitude reporting accuracy must be verified within ±125 feet per Part 43 Appendix F.

questions.learningObjective: Understand ELT operational test procedure
questions.fullObjective: ELT operational tests are limited to three audible sweeps (approximately one second) and may only be performed during the first five minutes of each hour per 91.207(d).

jeopardy.question: An ELT inspection per 91.207 requires verifying battery expiration, proper mounting, antenna condition, and this — which must be performed only during the first five minutes of each hour to avoid triggering a false alert to search-and-rescue satellites.
choices: A full-duration 60-second transmission test on 406 MHz
A brief operational test limited to three audible sweeps on 121.5 MHz
A 30-second transmission test monitored on the aircraft's comm radio
No transmission test — only a visual inspection of the cockpit-mounted status light
jeopardy.explanation: ELT operational tests are limited to three audible sweeps (approximately one second) and may only be performed during the first five minutes of each hour per 91.207(d).

questions.learningObjective: Understand Circuit breaker purpose
questions.fullObjective: Circuit breakers protect wiring — they are sized to the wire gauge, not the equipment.

jeopardy.question: In a 28VDC aircraft electrical system, this protective device opens the circuit when current exceeds its rated value — protecting the wiring from overheating, not the connected equipment.
choices: A voltage regulator
A current limiter relay
A circuit breaker
A zener diode clamp
jeopardy.explanation: Circuit breakers protect wiring — they are sized to the wire gauge, not the equipment.

multipleChoice.question: In a 28VDC aircraft electrical system, this protective device opens the circuit when current exceeds its rated value — protecting the wiring from overheating, not the connected equipment.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Tripped breaker response procedure
questions.fullObjective: A circuit breaker that trips immediately after reset indicates a hard short or sustained overload. The correct action is to leave it pulled and investigate.

jeopardy.question: After pulling and resetting a tripped circuit breaker once, it trips again immediately. Per standard aircraft maintenance practice, the correct next action is this.
choices: Replace the circuit breaker with a higher-rated unit to prevent nuisance trips
Reset it one more time — breakers are allowed two resets before grounding the circuit
Install a jumper wire to bypass the breaker and continue the maintenance run
Leave the breaker pulled and troubleshoot the protected circuit for a short or overload
jeopardy.explanation: A circuit breaker that trips immediately after reset indicates a hard short or sustained overload. The correct action is to leave it pulled and investigate.

questions.learningObjective: Understand WAAS LPV approach capability
questions.fullObjective: WAAS enables LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approaches — providing both lateral and vertical guidance to minimums as low as 200 feet, comparable to ILS CAT I. LNAV is non-precision (lateral only). VOR/DME overlays don't use WAAS. CAT III requires ILS ground equipment and certified autopilot systems.

jeopardy.question: WAAS corrects GPS errors caused by ionospheric delays, satellite clock drift, and orbital position inaccuracies — enabling this type of precision approach that replaces traditional ILS at many airports.
choices: RNAV (GPS) LNAV-only approach
LPV approach with vertical guidance to 200-foot minimums
Standard VOR/DME overlay approach
CAT III autoland approach with zero-visibility minimums
jeopardy.explanation: WAAS enables LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approaches — providing both lateral and vertical guidance to minimums as low as 200 feet, comparable to ILS CAT I. questions.learningObjective: Understand GPS ephemeris data
questions.fullObjective: Ephemeris data describes each satellite's precise orbital position and is essential for the GPS receiver's triangulation calculations.

jeopardy.question: Before a GPS receiver can compute a navigation solution, it must determine each satellite's precise orbital position using this data, which is broadcast by each satellite and updated approximately every two hours.
choices: NOTAM schedule data
WAAS ionospheric correction model
Ephemeris data
FMS waypoint database

questions.learningObjective: Understand WAAS vertical guidance loss
questions.fullObjective: "LNAV only" means the system has lateral guidance but has lost WAAS vertical guidance — likely due to loss of the WAAS geostationary satellite signal or integrity alert.

jeopardy.question: A GPS-equipped aircraft is cleared for an LPV approach, but the WAAS annunciation shows "LNAV only" during the approach. This indicates the system has lost this critical capability.
choices: DME distance measurement to the runway threshold
Radar altimeter input for decision height verification
ILS glideslope frequency auto-tuning
Vertical guidance from WAAS geostationary satellite corrections
jeopardy.explanation: "LNAV only" means the system has lateral guidance but has lost WAAS vertical guidance — likely due to loss of the WAAS geostationary satellite signal or integrity alert.

questions.learningObjective: Understand RAIM prediction requirement
questions.fullObjective: RAIM uses redundant satellites to detect a faulty satellite signal. Non-WAAS GPS approaches require RAIM prediction during preflight.

jeopardy.question: This autonomous integrity monitoring function within the GPS receiver predicts whether satellite geometry will be adequate to support the planned approach — and must be checked during preflight for RNAV (GPS) approaches without WAAS.
choices: RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring)
FDE (Fault Detection and Exclusion)
SBAS (Satellite Based Augmentation System)
ABAS (Aircraft Based Augmentation System)

questions.learningObjective: Understand DME slant-range measurement
questions.fullObjective: DME measures slant-range distance by timing paired pulse round-trips.

jeopardy.question: Unlike VOR which provides bearing, DME measures this — calculated by timing the round-trip of paired radio pulses between the aircraft and a ground station.
choices: Magnetic heading to the station
Slant-range distance in nautical miles
Groundspeed corrected for wind
Station frequency identification

questions.fullObjective: ADF receives NDB stations on low and medium frequencies (190–535 kHz). These frequencies are susceptible to atmospheric and terrain effects.

L-band (1575.42 MHz)

questions.learningObjective: Understand Airborne weather radar capability
questions.fullObjective: Airborne weather radar (AWR) actively transmits X-band energy and detects precipitation returns. It cannot detect CAT because clear air turbulence involves no moisture.

jeopardy.question: This airborne weather detection system transmits its own X-band radar energy forward of the aircraft and paints precipitation returns on a cockpit display — but cannot detect clear air turbulence.

Stormscope lightning detection system
NEXRAD datalink weather overlay
Satellite weather uplink (SXM/FIS-B)

questions.learningObjective: Understand DME frequency offset purpose
questions.fullObjective: DME uses a frequency offset (63 MHz) between interrogation and reply channels so the airborne receiver does not mistake its own transmitted pulses for ground station replies.

jeopardy.question: A DME interrogator sends pulse pairs on one frequency and listens for replies on a different frequency — this frequency offset between transmit and receive prevents this self-interference problem.
choices: Co-channel ATC radar interrogation conflicts
Adjacent-channel VOR bearing errors
GPS multipath reflection contamination
The receiver triggering on its own transmitted pulses
jeopardy.explanation: DME uses a frequency offset (63 MHz) between interrogation and reply channels so the airborne receiver does not mistake its own transmitted pulses for ground station replies.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Audio ground loop diagnosis
questions.fullObjective: A 400 Hz hum in aircraft audio is the classic ground loop symptom — the aircraft AC system operates at 400 Hz.

jeopardy.question: When a newly installed audio panel produces a loud 400 Hz hum in all headsets, the most likely cause is this wiring fault in the audio ground reference.
choices: A ground loop between the audio panel chassis and the airframe ground bus
A reversed hot and neutral on the aircraft's AC essential bus
Excessive cable length exceeding the audio panel's rated output impedance
An incorrect squelch threshold setting on the installed comm radio

questions.learningObjective: Understand Audio impedance matching
questions.fullObjective: Impedance matching between the audio panel output and connected headsets/speakers prevents signal reflection, distortion, and volume issues.

jeopardy.question: During an audio panel installation, this measurement between the panel's audio output and the headset jack must match the specified value to prevent distortion or excessively low volume.
choices: DC voltage bias on the microphone input
RF noise floor measured at the antenna port
Impedance match between audio output and the connected load
Current draw from the audio amplifier circuit breaker

questions.learningObjective: Understand Comm 2 audio troubleshooting
questions.fullObjective: Comm 2 transmits normally (proving the radio and antenna function). The fault is the audio receive path between Comm 2's audio output and the audio panel's Comm 2 input pin.

jeopardy.question: In a dual-comm installation, the pilot's headset receives audio from Comm 1 but not Comm 2, even though Comm 2 transmits normally. The audio panel is set to receive both. The most likely fault is here.
choices: Comm 2's squelch circuit has failed open, blocking all received audio
The Comm 2 antenna has an open coaxial shield causing receive failure
The audio panel's Comm 2 volume potentiometer is set to minimum
The audio panel's Comm 2 receive wire or connection at the panel's input pin

questions.learningObjective: Understand ARINC 429 simplex architecture
questions.fullObjective: ARINC 429 is unidirectional — one transmitter sends to one or more receivers on a single twisted-pair bus.

jeopardy.question: Unlike Ethernet or MIL-STD-1553, an ARINC 429 data bus has only one transmitter per wire pair — meaning data flows in only one direction. This architecture is described as this.
choices: Unidirectional (simplex) point-to-multipoint
Bidirectional (full-duplex) peer-to-peer
Half-duplex bus-mastered with token passing
Multi-master with collision detection (CSMA/CD)
jeopardy.explanation: ARINC 429 is unidirectional — one transmitter sends to one or more receivers on a single twisted-pair bus.

questions.learningObjective: Understand ARINC 429 SSM field
questions.fullObjective: The SSM (bits 9-10) indicates data validity — Normal Operation, No Computed Data, Functional Test, or Failure Warning.

jeopardy.question: An ARINC 429 word is exactly 32 bits long. Bits 9 and 10 carry the Sign/Status Matrix (SSM), which tells the receiving system this critical information about the data in the word.
choices: The transmitting LRU's part number and software version
Whether the data is valid, a test value, or a failure warning
The bus speed (high or low) at which the word was transmitted
The encryption key required to decode the BCD/BNR payload
jeopardy.explanation: The SSM (bits 9-10) indicates data validity — Normal Operation, No Computed Data, Functional Test, or Failure Warning.

questions.learningObjective: Understand MIL-STD-1553 bus controller role
questions.fullObjective: MIL-STD-1553 uses a command/response protocol — the Bus Controller (BC) initiates all communications.

jeopardy.question: Unlike ARINC 429's single-transmitter architecture, MIL-STD-1553 allows up to 31 remote terminals on a shared bus — all controlled by this single device that initiates every data transfer.
choices: The highest-priority remote terminal
The broadcast monitor terminal
The bus controller
The standby redundancy manager
jeopardy.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 uses a command/response protocol — the Bus Controller (BC) initiates all communications.

questions.learningObjective: Understand MIL-STD-1553 dual bus redundancy
questions.fullObjective: Dual redundant buses provide hardware fault tolerance — if Bus A develops a physical fault, Bus B carries all traffic.

jeopardy.question: MIL-STD-1553 uses dual redundant buses (Bus A and Bus B) with transformer-coupled stubs. This redundancy design ensures continued operation when this occurs.
choices: The bus controller software encounters a runtime exception
More than 31 remote terminals are connected simultaneously
Data words exceed the 20-bit payload maximum
One bus suffers a physical fault such as an open or short
jeopardy.explanation: Dual redundant buses provide hardware fault tolerance — if Bus A develops a physical fault, Bus B carries all traffic.

questions.learningObjective: Understand RS-232 crossover wiring error
questions.fullObjective: Connecting TXD-to-TXD means both devices are transmitting on the same line and neither is listening.

jeopardy.question: When connecting an RS-232 port from a GPS receiver to an MFD, TXD from one device must connect to RXD on the other. If both TXD pins are connected together, this is the result.
choices: Data is received at half the configured baud rate
No data is received by either device
Both devices receive each other's data simultaneously
The higher-voltage device overrides the lower-voltage device
jeopardy.explanation: Connecting TXD-to-TXD means both devices are transmitting on the same line and neither is listening.

questions.learningObjective: Understand RS-232 to TTL level conversion
questions.fullObjective: RS-232 uses bipolar voltage swings, while internal avionics logic runs on TTL/CMOS levels. A level converter (such as MAX232) translates between the two.

jeopardy.question: RS-232 signal levels swing between positive and negative voltages (typically ±5V to ±12V), which makes them incompatible with this modern logic standard used inside digital avionics — requiring a level converter chip at the interface.
choices: Differential signaling used by ARINC 429
Current-loop signaling used by older teletype equipment
TTL/CMOS logic levels (0V and +3.3V or +5V)
Fiber-optic signaling used by AFDX networks
jeopardy.explanation: RS-232 uses bipolar voltage swings, while internal avionics logic runs on TTL/CMOS levels. A level converter (such as MAX232) translates between the two.

multipleChoice.question: RS-232 signal levels swing between positive and negative voltages (typically ±5V to ±12V), which makes them incompatible with this modern logic standard used inside digital avionics — requiring a level converter chip at the interface.
choices: Differential signaling used by ARINC 429
Current-loop signaling used by older teletype equipment
TTL/CMOS logic levels (0V and +3.3V or +5V)
Fiber-optic signaling used by AFDX networks
multipleChoice.explanation: RS-232 uses bipolar voltage swings, while internal avionics logic runs on TTL/CMOS levels. A level converter (such as MAX232) translates between the two.

questions.learningObjective: Understand ARINC 429 transmitter output verification
questions.fullObjective: ARINC 429 transmitters drive a differential voltage on the twisted pair. If the oscilloscope shows no voltage swing, the transmitter is not driving the bus.

jeopardy.question: When troubleshooting an ARINC 429 bus with no data output, verifying this physical measurement at the transmitter's output pins with an oscilloscope confirms whether the transmitting LRU is actually driving the bus.
choices: DC supply voltage at the LRU's power input connector
Ground continuity between the LRU chassis and airframe ground
The baud rate setting in the LRU configuration menu
A differential voltage swing (±5V high-speed or ±2.5V low-speed) on the twisted pair
jeopardy.explanation: ARINC 429 transmitters drive a differential voltage on the twisted pair. If the oscilloscope shows no voltage swing, the transmitter is not driving the bus.

questions.learningObjective: Understand SSM-based fault isolation
questions.fullObjective: SSM = "No Computed Data" means the AHRS is transmitting a properly formatted word but reporting it cannot compute a valid heading. The fault is internal to the AHRS.

jeopardy.question: A bus analyzer shows Label 310 (heading) arriving at the correct rate from the AHRS, but the PFD displays a red HDG flag. The label data shows SSM bits set to "No Computed Data." This tells the technician the fault is here.
choices: Inside the AHRS — it is transmitting but reporting that it cannot compute a valid heading
In the wiring between the AHRS and PFD — causing data corruption
In the PFD software — it is failing to parse a correctly formatted label
In the bus termination resistor — causing signal reflections that corrupt the SSM bits
jeopardy.explanation: SSM = "No Computed Data" means the AHRS is transmitting a properly formatted word but reporting it cannot compute a valid heading. The fault is internal to the AHRS.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Wire bundle separation requirements
questions.fullObjective: The aircraft manufacturer's IPC and wiring diagrams specify required separation distances, which vary by system and area.

jeopardy.question: Per AC 43.13-1B, wire bundles routed through pressurized areas must have this minimum clearance from flight control cables, hydraulic lines, and high-temperature ducting to prevent chafing, heat damage, and interference.
choices: No clearance required if a wrap of silicone tape is applied
A minimum separation distance specified in the aircraft's wiring diagram or IPC
One inch from all other aircraft systems regardless of installation
Contact is acceptable if anti-chafe conduit is installed over the bundle

questions.learningObjective: Understand Clamp overtightening damage
questions.fullObjective: Overtightened clamps crush conductors and damage insulation, creating high-resistance points and intermittent opens under vibration.

jeopardy.question: When routing a new wire bundle through an existing harness clamp, the clamp must not be overtightened to prevent this failure mode — which creates high-resistance connections and intermittent faults.
choices: Insulation discoloration from UV exposure
Conductor fatigue from vibration resonance
Conductor crushing that deforms the wire and damages insulation
Electrolytic corrosion from moisture ingress at connector backshells
jeopardy.explanation: Overtightened clamps crush conductors and damage insulation, creating high-resistance points and intermittent opens under vibration.

questions.learningObjective: Understand EMI shield purpose
questions.fullObjective: EMI shielding intercepts external electromagnetic fields and shunts them to ground before they can induce noise in the signal conductor.

jeopardy.question: The braided metal shield surrounding a sensitive signal wire in an avionics installation serves this primary purpose — and must be grounded per the installation drawing to be effective.
choices: Preventing external electromagnetic interference from coupling into the signal path
Providing a redundant ground return path for the signal current
Increasing the wire's current-carrying capacity by adding conductor area
Protecting the wire from physical abrasion during maintenance
jeopardy.explanation: EMI shielding intercepts external electromagnetic fields and shunts them to ground before they can induce noise in the signal conductor.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Alternator whine diagnosis
questions.fullObjective: Alternator whine varying with RPM is conducted EMI on the power bus, entering through the radio's DC power input.

jeopardy.question: After installing a new comm radio, the pilot reports alternator whine in the headset that varies with engine RPM. The most likely cause is EMI entering the audio system through this path.
choices: The VHF antenna picking up alternator harmonics on the comm frequency
The headset jack's internal microphone preamp oscillating due to impedance mismatch
The comm radio's internal power supply filter capacitor failing open
The power supply wiring — inadequate filtering or a missing ground on the radio's power input
jeopardy.explanation: Alternator whine varying with RPM is conducted EMI on the power bus, entering through the radio's DC power input.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Confined space atmospheric testing
questions.fullObjective: Confined space entry requires atmospheric testing with a calibrated 4-gas detector before and during entry.

jeopardy.question: Before entering an aircraft fuel tank or other confined space for avionics wiring inspection, FAA and OSHA regulations require completing this critical step to ensure the atmosphere is safe for entry.
choices: Running the aircraft APU for ventilation for 30 minutes before entry
Testing the atmosphere with a calibrated gas detector for oxygen level, flammable vapors, and toxic gases
Spraying the space with a degreasing agent to neutralize residual fumes
Verifying the aircraft insurance covers confined-space maintenance
jeopardy.explanation: Confined space entry requires atmospheric testing with a calibrated 4-gas detector before and during entry.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Soldering fume extraction
questions.fullObjective: Soldering fume extractors with activated carbon filters capture rosin flux decomposition products at the source.

jeopardy.question: A technician performing soldering work on avionics circuit boards should use this specific type of ventilation to prevent inhalation of flux fumes — which contain rosin decomposition products that can cause occupational asthma.
choices: An open shop door providing general building ventilation
A standard dust mask rated for particulate filtration only
A fume extractor with activated carbon filter positioned at the soldering point
A full-face respirator with organic vapor cartridges rated for 8-hour use
jeopardy.explanation: Soldering fume extractors with activated carbon filters capture rosin flux decomposition products at the source.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Flight line hearing protection
questions.fullObjective: Turbine engine noise routinely exceeds 130 dB — well above the 85 dB OSHA threshold. Noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent.

jeopardy.question: During engine run-up while troubleshooting avionics near the flight line, this PPE item is required because sustained exposure to turbine engine noise levels (typically 130+ dB) causes this type of irreversible occupational injury.
choices: Impact-rated safety glasses to prevent FOD eye injuries
Steel-toed boots to protect against dropped tooling
High-visibility vest for ramp traffic awareness
Hearing protection (earplugs or earmuffs) to prevent permanent noise-induced hearing loss
jeopardy.explanation: Turbine engine noise routinely exceeds 130 dB — well above the 85 dB OSHA threshold. Noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Evacuation power de-energization
questions.fullObjective: De-energizing all power sources removes potential ignition sources and electrical shock hazards for responding firefighters.

jeopardy.question: When evacuating a hangar during a fire alarm, technicians must do this with any aircraft power and test equipment before leaving — failure to do so significantly increases the risk of the fire spreading.
choices: Move the test equipment to the nearest emergency exit for later retrieval
Cover sensitive avionics with anti-static bags to prevent smoke damage
Disconnect or de-energize all aircraft and bench power sources
Lock the tool cabinet to prevent unauthorized access during evacuation
jeopardy.explanation: De-energizing all power sources removes potential ignition sources and electrical shock hazards for responding firefighters.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Lithium battery thermal runaway response
questions.fullObjective: Lithium battery fires are self-oxidizing, so CO₂ and halon (oxygen displacement) are ineffective. Water cools the cells below the thermal runaway temperature.

jeopardy.question: A lithium battery in an avionics unit enters thermal runaway, producing white smoke, intense heat, and a chemical fire. Standard CO₂ and dry chemical extinguishers are ineffective because lithium fires require this response.
choices: Flooding the area with halon gas to reduce oxygen concentration below 12%
Applying a standard ABC dry chemical extinguisher at maximum discharge rate
Smothering the fire with a standard fire blanket rated for Class B use
Applying copious water to cool the cells below the thermal runaway threshold
jeopardy.explanation: Lithium battery fires are self-oxidizing, so CO₂ and halon (oxygen displacement) are ineffective. Water cools the cells below the thermal runaway temperature.

A manufacturer's service bulletin compliance record

The customer's maintenance training records and qualifications

The original equipment manufacturer's technical support engineer
The FAA's principal avionics inspector assigned to the repair station
The repair station's designated inspector or quality control representative

questions.learningObjective: Understand CMOS ESD sensitivity threshold
questions.fullObjective: Many CMOS components can be damaged by ESD events as low as 100 volts or less. A human cannot feel a discharge below approximately 3,000 volts.

jeopardy.question: Static discharge as low as this voltage level can destroy a CMOS gate inside an avionics LRU — far below what a person can feel (approximately 3,000 volts).
choices: 100 volts or less
1,000 volts
2,500 volts
5,000 volts
jeopardy.explanation: Many CMOS components can be damaged by ESD events as low as 100 volts or less. A human cannot feel a discharge below approximately 3,000 volts.

questions.learningObjective: Understand ESD wrist strap resistance range
questions.fullObjective: ESD wrist straps contain a 1 MΩ resistor that limits current to safe levels while still dissipating static charge.

jeopardy.question: Before touching any circuit boards or static-sensitive components, a technician must verify that the resistance reading on their ESD wrist strap tester falls within this range — confirming the strap provides both static dissipation and personnel safety.
choices: Less than 1 ohm (direct short to ground)
Between 750 kΩ and 10 MΩ (controlled dissipation with current limiting)
Greater than 100 MΩ (high-impedance isolation from ground)
Exactly 0 ohms (zero resistance to facility ground)
jeopardy.explanation: ESD wrist straps contain a 1 MΩ resistor that limits current to safe levels while still dissipating static charge.

questions.learningObjective: Understand ESD-safe packaging for transport
questions.fullObjective: ESD bags are designed to safely transport static-sensitive items. Standard packaging materials are triboelectric generators.

jeopardy.question: When transporting an avionics LRU from the stockroom to the ESD-protected bench, the unit must remain inside this packaging material — which dissipates static charge rather than generating it.
choices: Standard cardboard shipping box
Clear polyethylene bubble wrap
Static-dissipative or conductive ESD bag (pink or silver/black)
Foam peanuts in a sealed plastic bin
jeopardy.explanation: ESD bags are designed to safely transport static-sensitive items. Standard packaging materials are triboelectric generators.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Latent ESD damage
questions.fullObjective: Latent ESD damage weakens semiconductor junctions without immediately destroying them. The component passes testing but has reduced reliability.

jeopardy.question: An ESD-damaged component may pass initial bench testing but fail prematurely in service. This type of ESD failure — where the damage weakens but does not immediately destroy the component — accounts for the majority of field ESD-related failures.
choices: Catastrophic failure detected during acceptance testing
Manufacturing defect unrelated to handling
Electromagnetic interference from adjacent installed equipment
Latent defect — degraded but functional until stress causes later failure
jeopardy.explanation: Latent ESD damage weakens semiconductor junctions without immediately destroying them. The component passes testing but has reduced reliability.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Tool accountability procedure
questions.fullObjective: Tool accountability — counting every tool before and after the job — is the primary FOD prevention measure.

jeopardy.question: Before and after every maintenance task inside an aircraft, technicians must perform this accountability procedure — which is the single most effective method of preventing foreign object damage.
choices: A complete tool inventory count matching the tool control sheet
A visual sweep of the work area with a flashlight
A supervisor walkthrough signoff on the FOD prevention checklist
A borescope inspection of all accessible compartments
jeopardy.explanation: Tool accountability — counting every tool before and after the job — is the primary FOD prevention measure.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Dropped fastener FOD response
questions.fullObjective: A dropped fastener is a known FOD hazard. It must be retrieved before work continues and before any panels are closed.

jeopardy.question: A technician drops a small screw inside an avionics bay behind the instrument panel. The required response per Part 145 shop procedures is this — regardless of how difficult retrieval may be.
choices: Note it in the maintenance record and release the aircraft with the known FOD
Stop work immediately and retrieve the item before continuing — do not close any panels until it is found
Continue working and retrieve it during the next scheduled inspection
Notify the customer and let them decide whether retrieval is necessary
jeopardy.explanation: A dropped fastener is a known FOD hazard. It must be retrieved before work continues and before any panels are closed.

questions.learningObjective: Understand FOD containment tools
questions.fullObjective: Containment is a core FOD prevention principle. Loose hardware placed on a wing surface or floor can migrate into the aircraft.

jeopardy.question: FOD pouches, magnetic trays, and parts containers placed within arm's reach during aircraft maintenance serve this specific purpose in the FOD prevention program.
choices: Organizing parts by size for faster installation during reassembly
Protecting removed parts from ESD damage on the shop floor
Containing loose hardware and fasteners so they cannot fall into the aircraft structure
Labeling removed components for warranty tracking purposes
jeopardy.explanation: Containment is a core FOD prevention principle. Loose hardware placed on a wing surface or floor can migrate into the aircraft.

questions.learningObjective: Understand Process-generated FOD debris
questions.fullObjective: Process-generated debris is the most insidious FOD category because it is created by the maintenance work itself.

jeopardy.question: Safety wire clippings, stripped wire insulation, cable tie tails, and solder splashes are all examples of this category of FOD that is uniquely hazardous because individual pieces are small enough to be overlooked but can cause electrical shorts or mechanical jams.
choices: Consumable supply contamination
Tool accountability violations
Chemical contamination requiring hazmat cleanup
Process-generated debris — byproducts of the maintenance work itself
jeopardy.explanation: Process-generated debris is the most insidious FOD category because it is created by the maintenance work itself.
